positive_text: Who should buy: A successor to Adidas' 3D midsole saga, the 4DFWD wears its innovative 3D-printed cushioning on its dreamy knit sleeve. Add this shoe to your shopping cart if you want a pair that catches the eyes of many, whether in a casual or running setting, and if futuristic sneakers are your thing. Pros: Bouncier than most 4D shoes, comfortable in-shoe feel, wider-than-average sole, very flexible, durable outsole, innovative cushioning tech, attention grabber, sustainable.
negative_text: Who should not buy: If you prefer a lighter and more affordable pair of Adidas running shoes, consider the Adizero Boston 11. If you are concerned about the upper stretching out too much over time, look at the 4DFWD v2 instead because it comes with a more structured upper. Cons: Upper stretches a lot over time, lacks breathability, rather heavy.

positive_text: Who should buy: This distinctive offering from Adidas is a solid option to consider if you are a sneakerhead and want a running shoe that looks, and indeed is, very cool, for your casual and daily runs, or to use all day. Pros: Unique midsole construction, supportive fit, comfortable in-shoe feel, good grip on roads, extremely durable, plenty flexible, stunning design, environmental-friendly.
negative_text: Who should not buy: If you want an affordable option, the Adidas Adistar is a better choice. If you need something lighter instead, the Adidas Adizero Boston 11 is about 80 g lighter in a men's US 9. Cons: Not for the budget-minded, very heavy, not for wide feet, some breaking-in required.

positive_text: Who should buy: The Adidas 4DFWD 3 is an excellent choice for neutral runners in the market for a well-cushioned and flexible daily trainer for low-intensity runs, runners in cooler climates looking for a shoe that will keep their feet warm and perform consistently all year round, and style-minded individuals looking for an eye-catching exercise shoe that's also good for strutting about town. Pros: Extremely comfortable, excellent traction, great 4D midsole cushion, durable upper and outsole, flexible and forgiving on the foot, secure lockdown, works great as a walking shoe, warm and consistent in the cold, eye-catching design, lots of colorway options.
negative_text: Who should not buy: With its lackluster performance in the breathability test, the 4DFWD 3 is not very well suited for warm weather runs; the Allbirds Tree Dasher 2.0 is better ventilated. At 12.3 oz (348 g), the 4DFWD 3 is not as light as its appearance suggests; the ASICS Dynablast 3 is a much lighter and more versatile option for runners who are more serious about running. The toebox may prove a little too snug for runners with broad feet; the Adidas Switch FWD is a roomier alternative. Overpronators will not find the stability they need with the 4DFWD and should look into options like the Brooks Adrenaline GTS 23 or Nike Structure 25 instead. Cons: Quite heavy, not good for high speeds or long distances, below average breathability, relatively narrow toebox.

positive_text: Who should buy: If you're any of the following, don't write off the Adistar from Adidas: neutral runners looking for a shoe that's stable and guides the foot, road runners who want a daily trainer with plenty of padding, runners who want a protective shoe for recovery days and long runs, and heel strikers who want to change their running stride. Pros: Stable landings, maxed-out cushion, relaxed and accommodating fit, roomy toe box, tongue stays in place, durable, summer-ready breathability, recycled upper, grippy on ice, great for long runs.
negative_text: Who should not buy: Adidas fans who want something lighter and cushier can go for the Adidas Ultraboost Light. Runners who want a soft yet responsive ride can look at the Nike Invincible Run 3 or Hoka Clifton 9, and heel strikers who want a more forgiving rearfoot can opt for the Saucony Triumph 20 instead. Cons: Could be more responsive, firm for heel strikers, heavier than most trainers, becomes a brick in winter.

positive_text: Who should buy: This workhorse from Adidas is for runners in search of a max-cushioned shoe for long and easy miles, a neutral shoe with a touch of support as a semi-stability shoe, a design that's stylish and comfortable enough for casual wear, and a daily trainer that doesn't break the bank because Adistar 2.0 costs $15 less than similar shoes. Pros: Cushioned for long miles, supportive for a neutral shoe, wide platform, secure foothold, well-padded interiors, highly breathable, great for casual wear, value for money, true to size.
negative_text: Who should not buy: The Adidas Adistar 2.0 is very heavy at 11.6 oz / 328 g even for its max-cushioned kind, and there are shoes with the same heel stack that weigh nearly 100 g less, such as the Adidas Adizero SL and PUMA Velocity Nitro 2. If you are a heel striker, you may also prefer a plusher foam under the heel, like the Adidas Ultraboost Light. Cons: Heavier than average, firm heel landings.

positive_text: Who should buy: The Adidas Adistar 3 is for fans of the previous Adistar models who demanded a lighter, more cushioned version because it delivers on both fronts, weekend warriors hitting the pavement 1 or 2 times a week who prioritize comfort in a running shoe, and budget-conscious buyers looking for a plush daily trainer that doubles as a comfortable walking shoe with a breathable upper. Pros: Amazing weight reduction, better running experience than v2, reasonably priced, suitable for walking, cushions long runs, breathable upper, exceptional all-around comfort.
negative_text: Who should not buy: While the Adistar 3 offers several improvements over its predecessor, its stability has significantly decreased, making it a less appealing option for runners needing good support; more reliable mild-stability trainers include the ASICS GT 2000 12, Hoka Arahi 7, and ASICS Gel Kayano 31. Cons: No more Continental, lacks energy return, upper needs better durability.

positive_text: Who should buy: The Adidas Adizero Adios 7 is a solid choice for anyone that needs a not-so-expensive but fast shoe, fans of old-school racing flats, and 5K/10K aficionados that want to do shorter-than-mile repeats on the track. Pros: Snappy ride, great for fast track workouts, well ventilated, amazing ground feel, lighter than ever, the outsole lasts forever, grippy Continental rubber, modestly priced.
negative_text: Who should not buy: This shoe offers an energetic and potentially enjoyable ride, but it can also be physically demanding on the muscles. If you typically train in shoes with modern, cushioned foams, this racing flat may not be the ideal choice for fast workouts or races, especially given the availability of the highly acclaimed and Lightstrike-Pro-based Adidas Adizero Adios Pro 3. Cons: Not for cold weather, heel slippage, needs more Lightstrike Pro, ride is too firm.

positive_text: Who should buy: The Adios 8 is a great choice for runners who want to go fast but do not enjoy the rigidity of carbon-plated shoes, prefer more ground-feel underfoot with a lower stack and cushioning that feels balanced, want a race-ready shoe that will not break the bank, go out for runs in the summertime or in hot climates, and have a wider foot. Pros: Extremely breathable, snappy and speedy ride, suitable for tempo runs and long distances, fits wider feet, secure lockdown, grippy and durable outsole, budget friendly for a race-ready shoe at $130, ideal for forefoot strikers.
negative_text: Who should not buy: The more traditional sensation of the Adios 8’s midsole cushion means it does not provide the assistance or cruising capabilities of modern midsoles; for a speedy shoe with more technology underfoot, the Saucony Endorphin Speed 3 is a better option. Heel-striking or long-distance runners will also be better served with more foam underfoot and a slightly higher heel drop, such as the Saucony Endorphin Speed 3 or ASICS Metaspeed Sky+. Runners with a pronating stride should consider the Brooks Launch GTS 10 instead. Cons: Could be lighter, not for harsh winter days, noisy.

positive_text: Who should buy: The Adios 9 is the perfect shoe for runners who miss old-school racing flats with low stacks and zero plates, those wanting a tempo-ready shoe that feels fast and natural, lightweight midfoot strikers seeking a shoe for fast-paced efforts with massive energy return, and fans of the Adizero Adios 8 because version 9 is a huge upgrade. Pros: Full Lightstrike Pro superfoam, incredibly lightweight and fun, great for tempo sessions, works as a plateless 5K/10K racer, durable and grippy outsole, excellent value, ideal for track workouts.
negative_text: Who should not buy: The Adios 9 is not the best choice for runners needing generous cushioning because its low-stack build feels ultra-connected to the ground and may fall short for those wanting the ultimate leg-saving experience; the Adizero EVO SL is a better fit for that. It is also not recommended for runners who expect a super shoe-like experience because it lacks the rigid, carbon-plated snap that defines that category; for a more race-ready feel in a low-stack format, the Nike Streakfly 2 or New Balance FuelCell SuperComp Pacer v2 are better alternatives. Cons: Not stable at all, tapered toebox, not ideal for heel strikers, upper lacks airflow.

positive_text: Who should buy: Buy this shoe if you want a top-end race day shoe to squeeze your best effort out of. It is fast, fairly comfortable, decently priced, and proven to win world records. If you are a three-stripe fan and want a super shoe alternative to the usual choice, this is the one. Pros: Breathable, responsive, carbon rods, soft midsole, laces stay tight, price.
negative_text: Who should not buy: Do not buy this shoe if you have super wide feet, if you do not need the top-of-the-line but still want a fast modern race shoe and would rather check out the Adidas Adizero Boston 12, or if you just want a fun, bouncy, light shoe but do not need it to be carbon plated and would rather try the New Balance FuelCell Rebel v2. Cons: Runs narrow, unstable at slow speeds.

positive_text: Who should buy: The Adidas Adizero Adios Pro 3 is an excellent pick for any race from a short 5K to the marathon. It is designed for midfoot or forefoot strikers looking for a reliable racing shoe for upcoming races spanning from the 5K to the marathon, runners on the hunt for a top-tier racing shoe that offers stability without the lateral wobble often found in racing models, and fans of the Adios Pro series with wide feet who need enhanced stability and felt the previous editions too narrow. It also excels in interval training thanks to its long-lasting outsole and durable midsole. Pros: Supreme stability in a supershoe, world-class energy return and cushioning, feather-light and agile, a dream come true for marathoners, wonderful breathability, top-notch grip even in wet conditions, outstanding outsole durability, accommodates wide feet.
negative_text: Who should not buy: Diehard fans of the Adizero Adios Pro 2.0 may want to stick with their current model rather than upgrade because version 3 is significantly wider and caters more to midfoot strikers, while version 2 suits heel strikers better due to its high drop. Those with a preference for extremely plush racing shoes might also find the Adios Pro 3 disappointing because it feels somewhat firmer than competitors like the Nike Alphafly 2 or Saucony Endorphin Pro 3. Cons: Risk of heel slippage, lacing can be tricky, not suited for heel strikers.

positive_text: Who should buy: The highly anticipated Adios Pro 4 is an ideal choice for runners who found the Adios Pro 3 too firm, disliked its upper, or struggled with its heel lockdown, anyone in need of a responsive-and-grippy super shoe that excels across every race distance from 5K to marathons, midfoot and forefoot strikers looking for a plush super shoe, and runners who prefer a more flexible racing shoe over ultra-stiff options. Pros: Softer and bouncier Lightstrike Pro foam, maintains the same price, no more heel slippage, versatile from 5K to marathon distances, durable outsole with outstanding grip, fantastic for midfoot and forefoot strikers, improved for faster runners, comfortable gusseted tongue, lighter than Adios Pro 3.
negative_text: Who should not buy: The Adios Pro 4 introduces some drawbacks worth considering. The fit now features a tapered toebox design that sacrifices the wide-foot comfort of the Adios Pro 3, so runners concerned about that may prefer the Nike Alphafly 3. Heel strikers may find the Adios Pro 4 really unstable due to its exceptionally narrow heel and may be better off with the Saucony Endorphin Pro 4. For runners who find the price or stiffness of traditional super shoes off-putting, the Adidas Adizero EVO SL is a smarter choice. Cons: Unstable for every heel striker, narrow tapered toebox, upper lacks breathability, midsole loses bounce quicker than AP3.

positive_text: Who should buy: Based on our testing of the Adidas Adizero Adios Pro Evo 2, we think it works well for those who do not worry about budget and simply want Adidas’ latest hypershoe, marathoners who want an extremely lightweight shoe and prioritize grams above all else, and runners who benefit from a strong forefoot rocker and feel more efficient with this geometry. Pros: Insanely lightweight; fast and responsive; aggressive, efficient rocker; breathable, paper-thin upper; feels incredible at faster paces; specifically built for the marathon distance; uncompressed Lightstrike Pro Evo foam; ideal for midfoot and forefoot strikers.
negative_text: Who should not buy: In our view the insane price of the Adios Pro Evo 2 makes no sense for 99.9% of runners, even if the tech behind the shoe looks impressive. We believe there are better choices which deliver similar or even better performance at a lower cost. From our perspective the limited durability also turns this ultra-premium, race-only model into a tough sell, and we think it’s not the best choice when other shoes strike a smarter balance of cost, longevity and performance. Cons: Crazy high price; extremely narrow toebox; low durability; we expected a bit more bounce!

positive_text: Who should buy: Consider choosing the Adidas Adizero Boston 11 if you struggle with stability and need a shoe that can handle a wide range of paces, are looking for a durable shoe that can handle races and don't mind it being heavier than racing shoes, and seek a cushioned-yet-firm ride for long-distance training sessions. Pros: Very stable for being a high-stack shoe; excellent grip; breathable upper; versatile for different workouts; protective underfoot; extremely durable outsole; made with recycled materials; attractive design.
negative_text: Who should not buy: If you prefer plush or even moderately soft foams, we suggest staying away from the Boston 11. The same advice goes for those in search of flexible running shoes, as this one leans towards firmness and rigidity with its Energyrods. For moderate-to-slow paces, consider a fast-and-light daily trainer that comfortably handles a wide range of speeds; if a flexible tempo trainer is what you're after, this may not be your match. Cons: Definitely stiff and firm; long breaking-in period.

positive_text: Who should buy: The upgraded Adidas Adizero Boston 12 is a truly impressive shoe, and here's who we think will love it the most: midfoot-to-forefoot strikers looking for a training partner with outstanding grip; those who are fans of the Boston series but felt let down by the heavier weight of versions 10 and 11; and any runner in need of a versatile shoe that can handle all sorts of training runs, regardless of how fast or long they might be. Pros: Delivers a fun ride; lighter than versions 10 and 11; handles both slow and fast paces with ease; wide toebox; durable and grippy Continental outsole; even more Lightstrike Pro superfoam; improved stability due to a broader base; fantastic for midfoot-to-forefoot strikers.
negative_text: Who should not buy: If you're in the market for a soft, flexible running experience, we believe the Boston 12 might not be your shoe. While it's softer than its predecessor, it still delivers a somewhat firm and stiff feel underfoot. We also think there are better options if you plan to use the shoe for pure racing or track workouts. It's a bit too heavy for those purposes, and if budget isn't a concern there may be superior picks; if you're looking for a budget-friendly shoe for fast runs or short races, there are other options to consider. Cons: Upper durability falls short; tongue and heel could benefit from extra padding; heel strikers might prefer v11.

positive_text: Who should buy: After testing the Adizero Boston 13 to its limits, we can recommend it for all runners who enjoyed the Boston 12, as it’s a similar ride with a lighter build, better upper, and improved comfort; midfoot and forefoot strikers needing a stiff shoe that works wonders for tempo runs and the occasional race; and fans of Adidas super shoes looking for a solid training partner that won’t empty their wallets. Pros: Keeps losing weight; midfoot-friendly geometry; more Lightstrike Pro; breathable upper; redesigned heel counter improves lockdown; outsole grips well and lasts forever; stellar value for a supertrainer; ideal for tempo workouts.
negative_text: Who should not buy: The carbon-based Energy Rods 2.0 make this shoe stiff, and definitely not for runners who dislike that rigid, plate-like feel. We also believe heel strikers looking for extra rearfoot stability won’t enjoy the Boston 13. Lastly, the tapered toebox may cause discomfort on long runs, and some runners may want more room for their toes. Cons: Narrow heel limits stability; pointy toebox restricts toe splay; still too much Lightstrike 2.0?

positive_text: Who should buy: Despite its limited early release, we managed to buy a pair of the Adizero EVO SL, and after dissecting it and testing every detail, we recommend it for beginners wanting a do-it-all shoe for training and racing that offers reliable performance at an unbeatable price, anyone seeking a speedy, durable and fun training partner, runners who dislike carbon plates but crave world-class performance and grip, and those searching for a versatile shoe with premium foam without breaking the bank. Pros: 100% Lightstrike Pro superfoam; exceptional value-to-performance ratio; low weight; ultra-breathable upper; unmatched versatility; agile and fun ride; handles short and long runs with ease; striking Evo 1-inspired aesthetics.
negative_text: Who should not buy: The EVO SL impressed us in many ways, but we think it’s not the best choice for runners with stability concerns. Its narrow midsole and lack of support make it unsuitable for those needing even mild support. Additionally, we know this shoe won’t satisfy runners seeking the sensation of a plate, since the EVO SL is designed for a more natural ride. Finally, the limited early release by Adidas might make it impossible to spend your money on this shoe, and if it’s unavailable but you’re seeking a similar experience, you may need alternatives. Cons: Poor-quality laces; short, non-gusseted tongue; toebox durability.

positive_text: Who should buy: In our opinion, the Adizero EVO SL ATR suits perfectly fans of the Adizero EVO SL who struggled with its poor grip and protection in wet conditions, runners who liked the original model but wanted more confidence beyond perfectly paved roads, anyone looking for a cold-ready daily trainer with very high energy return that still comes at a reasonable price, and runners in cold climates who want one shoe for everyday runs and daily use without giving up speed or performance. Pros: Impressive energy return and shock absorption; premium-quality laces, finally; top traction thanks to updated Conti rubber; ideal for winter training and cold conditions; water-repellent upper; still delivers insane value; works well across a wide mix of surfaces; versatile for many paces; feels a bit more stable than the regular edition.
negative_text: Who should not buy: We’ve observed that the EVO SL ATR’s heat-trapping upper works great in winter, but we do not recommend it for warm weather. In our experience, the low airflow becomes uncomfortable fast. We’re under the impression that the ATR label can be misleading for some runners, and we think it’s not the best choice for serious trail running, as the shallow-lug outsole only suits very mild paths. Cons: Noticeably heavier than the regular EVO SL; virtually no ventilation; significantly reduced flexibility; limited heel stability.

positive_text: Who should buy: We've concluded that the Adidas Adizero Prime X 2 Strung is an outstanding choice for those who enjoyed the fun and bouncy ride of the original Prime X but felt it too unstable, as this update delivers enhanced support; marathoners unconcerned with World Athletics regulations who prioritize ultimate cushioning and technology regardless of cost and rules; and anyone with at least $300 in the bank to invest in top-tier engineering, since we’re convinced the technology behind the upper, midsole, and outsole make this shoe a real masterpiece. Pros: Amazing Strung upper; impressive durability; rule-breaker cushioning; integrated dual carbon plates; top energy return; exceptional Continental outsole; wonderful for long distance training; stability exceeds expectations; superior build quality; screams innovation.
negative_text: Who should not buy: The Prime X 2 Strung really showcases what Adidas is capable of, but it's a double-edged sword because of its high weight. It's clear for us that this shoe isn't the best choice for short races, and we're hesitant to recommend it even for half or full marathons. For a thrilling and unique running experience, some may prefer other models that offer a similar ride with much less bulk. Moreover, the steep price tag of the Prime X 2 Strung may deter many runners, and there are better choices out there for those seeking a max-cushioned running shoe. Cons: Simply too heavy; exorbitantly priced at $300; less exhilarating than v1.

positive_text: Who should buy: We recommend the Adidas Adizero Prime X3 STRUNG for runners with plenty of room in the budget who want a shoe loaded with next-level foam and cutting-edge tech, Adidas fans looking for the brand’s top-tier innovation all wrapped into one model, those chasing a maximalist shoe with sky-high shock absorption and energetic bounce, and loyalists of the Prime X who felt the second version lost its spark, because this one brings back a bit of v1's magic but with a calmer, more stable ride. Pros: Excellent shock absorption; fantastic bounce from new Lightstrike Pro foam; fits better than v2; innovative tech and design from heel to toe; STRUNG upper is just awesome; works well on any season; skyscraper-like stack height; a total blast if it suits you; breathable upper.
negative_text: Who should not buy: We think the Prime X3 STRUNG falls short as a race-day option, and most carbon-plated super shoes offer similar shock absorption in a lighter, more race-ready build. Additionally, we believe that even as a high-tech super trainer, the price tag will be too high for most. With other options delivering similar performance at a much lower cost, the Prime X3 feels like a niche pick that only makes sense if price isn't a concern at all. Cons: Crazy expensive; heavier than all supershoes; heel strikers may feel unstable; too stiff for easy days.

positive_text: Who should buy: In our view, the Adidas Adizero SL is an excellent choice for beginners embarking on their running journey and seeking an all-rounder, individuals who find the Pegasus too narrow and desire a wider alternative, and runners who appreciate firmer midsoles and need a durable running shoe. Pros: Spacious upper; durable outsole; highly breathable mesh; surprisingly light; stable for being a neutral daily trainer; great value; suitable for track workouts.
negative_text: Who should not buy: If a soft, cloud-like midsole is your thing, steer clear of the Adizero SL because it's on the firmer side. For heel strikers who require added stability, this shoe might not work out either, because it's just too narrow. Cons: Lightstrike feels like a brick; low energy return in the heel; heel is extremely narrow.

positive_text: Who should buy: We're impressed with the Adizero SL2, and we believe it's a great pick for anyone seeking a lightweight running shoe versatile enough for any kind of run, including track workouts; fans of the original SL2 who crave a more responsive midsole without sacrificing the beloved fit and affordability; and neutral runners who prioritize speed and agility over stability in their daily running shoe. Pros: Lightweight; enhanced with more Lightstrike Pro superfoam; suits various running paces; can double as a racer for beginners; spacious fit; highly breathable; excellent value; surprisingly plush upper; grippy even in rain.
negative_text: Who should not buy: We do not recommend the Adidas Adizero SL2 for those in search of a maximalist daily trainer with ample cushioning, particularly heavier runners. Additionally, the SL2’s midsole might let down runners expecting full-length Lightstrike Pro, and those users may prefer alternatives that deliver exactly that. Cons: Tongue design needs enhancement; outsole durability; not very stable; not the best for heel strikers.

positive_text: Who should buy: Midfoot and forefoot strikers will find its massive energy return and aggressive grip perfect for shaving seconds off all 5K and 10K races. Track enthusiasts will appreciate the spike-like feel—close to the ground and highly responsive—yet with enhanced leg protection. Admirers of the Takumi Sen legacy will be pleased too, as the 10th edition maintains its hallmark attributes—it’s lightweight, narrow, and built for speed. Pros: Lightweight; Awesome for 5K/10K races; Responsive Lightstrike Pro midsole; Breathable engineered mesh; Loves to go fast; Awesome for track workouts; Continental rubber; Amazing at taking corners; Recycled materials in the upper.
negative_text: Who should not buy: The Takumi Sen 10 is not suited for rearfoot strikers or those with wider feet—it has a super-narrow heel and a cramped toebox and we found the heel stability lacking, which might pose a problem for many runners. We also think the Takumi Sen 10 has an suboptimal cushioning-to-weight ratio, and runners looking for substantial foam underfoot will find better options in pricier models. Cons: Not for heel strikers; Durability concerns; Extremely cramped fit.

positive_text: Who should buy: A solid choice for long-time fans of the Takumi Sen line. Perfect for midfoot and forefoot strikers doing track workouts, short intervals, and fast road sessions. An excellent super shoe option for lightweight runners, especially women, who do not need or want a max-stack racing shoe. Pros: Exceptionally fast feel; Perfect for 5K and 10K races; Elite-level energy return; Excellent upper durability; Competitive price; Stays true to the Takumi Sen DNA; Great match for lightweight runners; Outstanding grip.
negative_text: Who should not buy: We think the Takumi Sen 11 is not the best choice for marathon runners because of its low stack height. We also believe the low airflow can be a problem for some runners and do not recommend it if your feet get hot easily. Cons: Very limited heel stability; Not for beginners; Low breathability; Maybe a bit heavy for 2026 standards.

positive_text: Who should buy: Beginner runners looking for a neutral road shoe. Casual runners who want a versatile shoe that’s stylish and comfy enough for day to day use as well occasional easy runs or gym days. Sustainably minded individuals who will appreciate the use of recycled materials in the shoe’s upper. Pros: Great impact protection; Bounce midsole foam at the heel provides a premium feeling ride; Good for walking, gym days, as well as easy runs; Remarkably stable underfoot; Grippy and durable outsole; Budget friendly; Stylish and sustainable design.
negative_text: Who should not buy: The Alphabounce+ is prohibitively heavy when attempting anything beyond an easy and short run. The shoe also has only average airflow and will feel quite toasty during runs on warmer days. Cons: Very heavy for a road shoe; Breathability could be better; Tapered toebox; Lacking in upper durability.

positive_text: Who should buy: Everyday runners who want a cheap running shoe that puts comfort at the forefront. Daily trainers for short to long miles on the road. Runners who need a versatile shoe for runs, all-day wear, walks, and gym training. Pros: Comfy for all-day wear; Lightweight for its kind; Stable even during sharp turns; Breezy on warm days; Perfect for beginners; Can handle gym training; Durable for the price; Soft upper; Affordable; Eco-friendly upper.
negative_text: Who should not buy: Runners with wide feet may need a break-in period, and runners wanting a springy ride for faster efforts should look elsewhere. Cons: Break-in period for wide feet; Lacks spring for fast runs.

positive_text: Who should buy: Beginners looking for their first running shoe that blends performance with durability. Runners who need a lightweight shoe with a spacious upper. Marathoners searching for a low-drop, cost-effective workhorse for racking up the miles. Pros: Awesome value; Ideal for midfoot strikers; Well-cushioned; Excellent breathability; Durable outsole; Lightweight; Spacious toebox; Surprisingly grippy.
negative_text: Who should not buy: The Duramo Speed features a 6.0-mm drop, which might not cater well to those who prefer high-drop shoes with over 8 mm of offset. For those with narrow feet, the Duramo Speed might not be the best fit. Cons: Somewhat limited versatility; Lacing system; Suboptimal for heel strikers; Lacks bounce.

positive_text: Who should buy: Budget-focused runners who want a lightweight build and a durable outsole. Fans of the first Duramo Speed who want a safe, familiar update with small refinements. Beginners running shorter distances who need a simple, reliable shoe at a low cost. Pros: Very affordable price; Lightweight build; Durable outsole; Solid traction on dry and wet surfaces; Big reflective elements; Stable for midfoot and forefoot strikers; Can be used for daily runs or tempo runs; Ideal for beginners.
negative_text: Who should not buy: The Duramo Speed 2 comes at a low price, but its energy return is clearly limited and runners who care about bounce may want something else. Runners planning to use it as a daily trainer may prefer a more durability-ready upper with greater vertical room in the toebox. Cons: Very low energy return; Not much shock absorption in the forefoot; Upper durability should be better; Limited heel stability.

positive_text: Who should buy: Beginner and casual runners looking for a catch-all exercise shoe. Runners looking for a shoe on a low budget. Neutral pronators who don’t require additional arch support. Pros: Bounce midsole mimics ride of more premium foams; Extremely durable for a budget shoe; Spacious toebox for wide feet; Generously padded for comfort; Suitable for running, walking, and indoor workouts; Subtle and stylish look for casual use; Wallet-friendly.
negative_text: Who should not buy: Serious runners looking for a speedier daily shoe, flat-footed runners who need stability features, and heel strikers who want more rearfoot cushioning should look elsewhere. Cons: Could be lighter; Limited arch support for flat feet; Squeaky outsole; Runs warm.

positive_text: Who should buy: Budget-conscious runners who crave a well-cushioned and stable shoe. Runners who want the softest ride in a budget-friendly Adidas shoe. Runners with wide feet. Pros: Ideal for wide feet; Well-padded midsole; Comfortable upper material; Suitable for winter runs; Stable; Great for beginners; Ideal for easy runs; Excellent value.
negative_text: Who should not buy: If heavy shoes aren't your thing, avoid the Galaxy 6 because it can feel like you're dragging your feet when you try to pick up the pace. Runners living in warmer climates might also want to pass because its breathability is one of the poorest tested. Cons: Subpar breathability; Excessively heavy; Poor traction.

positive_text: Who should buy: Runners who want massive cushioning with strong energy return for long runs. Runners who worry about outsole durability in super trainers and prefer full-length rubber coverage. Runners who love the Ultraboost line and are ready for the next step. Runners who prefer one versatile shoe for everything: short runs, long efforts, and even race day. Pros: Bouncy Hyperboost Pro foam; Massive shock absorption; A real state-of-the-art super trainer; Superior traction + full-length coverage; Excellent durability overall; Comfortable Primeweave upper; Feels agile compared to most competitors; Lightweight for its size; Ideal for long runs and even marathons.
negative_text: Who should not buy: The price of the Hyperboost Edge may feel high for many runners and it’s not the best choice if you’re looking for strong value for the money. It is also not recommended for runners who need extra toe space because its tapered toebox creates a race-snug fit that won’t suit wider feet. Cons: Limited breathability; Aesthetics may not appeal to everyone; Late forefoot rocker may feel awkward for some; Potential ankle rubbing from stiff upper.

positive_text: Who should buy: Beginners seeking a versatile option that effortlessly transitions between a running shoe and a cozy sneaker. Heel strikers on the hunt for a shoe featuring a plush yet thin midsole, complemented by a wide upper. Adidas enthusiasts who love the Boost technology and don't mind the additional weight. Pros: Breathable and comfortable upper; Doubles as a casual shoe or gym companion; Spacious toebox; Crafted for heel strikers; Performs excellently in cold weather; Remarkably stable, even at easy paces; Full-length plush Boost midsole; Excellent energy return.

positive_text: We recommend the ASICS Gel Excite 10 as a great choice for: Beginners in need of a neutral daily trainer for easy to moderate-difficulty runs Runners seeking a well-cushioned, breathable shoe that's reliable all year round Casual runners who want a versatile shoe that can also tackle other fitness activities Those in the market for a shoe that's comfy enough for all-day wear and walks around town Smooth transitions Flexible and easy on the foot Feels stable underfoot Performs consistently in the cold Breathable for warm days Durable, well-constructed upper Generously padded heel counter Secure lockdown Budget-friendly Suitable for walks and gym days
negative_text: The Gel Excite 10's ride is anything but exciting. While its stack ensures we were well-cushioned from impact during our test runs, the midsole lacks the lively rebound to support tempo training adequately. This isn't surprising for a shoe at this price point, and the more energetic Flyte Foam found in the ASICS Novablast 3 proves that you get what you pay for. However, the Brooks Revel 6 is a speedy daily trainer that we can recommend to beginners who don't want to break the bank. With an accurate drop of 11.8 mm based on our measurements, the Gel Excite 10's offset is too steep for forefoot strikers. We recommend that those runners check out the mid-drop Saucony Axon 2, or the Altra Rivera 2 with its 2.8 mm offset as alternatives more suited to their running gait. While the Gel Excite 10 feels quite stable underfoot, it lacks the support that overpronating runners require. We recommend the ASICS GT 1000 12 as a similarly budget-friendly option, or the pricier Gel Kayano 30 for those in need of a stability shoe. It is comfortable, responsive, and won’t take any time to break in or get used to, and it is a classic do-it-all type of training shoe that can fit a lot of different needs for a wide variety of runners. Pros: Lightweight stability shoe, responsive ride, comfortable, wide toe box, good lockdown, durable outsole, price.
negative_text: Who should not buy: This shoe is not for runners who want a higher-performing stability shoe, who don’t need stability control shoes, or who want a fast race day shoe. Cons: Overly padded tongue, thick ankle collar, runs warm, feels a touch long.

positive_text: Who should buy: The ASICS GT 2000 11 is an excellent choice for those who are looking for daily running shoes that are both supportive and comfortable, runners who need a daily trainer that’s both lightweight and responsive, and fans of the Cumulus 24 who have low arches or flat feet. Pros: A gentle stability shoe; well-cushioned; comfortable in-shoe feel; responsive ride; light for its kind; secure lockdown; breathable; great value for money.
negative_text: Who should not buy: This might be a pass for runners with severe overpronation; we found it better to consider the ASICS Gel Kayano 30 instead, and for those who need just a touch of stability, the neutral ASICS Gel Nimbus 24 is our recommended option. Cons: Narrower-than-expected toebox; overly padded tongue.

positive_text: Who should buy: We think the ASICS GT 2000 12 is an excellent pick for runners looking for a reliable daily trainer with a touch of stability support, particularly appealing to women who generally pronate a bit more than men, those seeking a versatile, not overly stacked shoe that does a great job in short and long runs and delivers consistent performance, and anyone in need of a comfortable, supportive shoe that offers a well-rounded experience at a great value. Pros: Stable yet non-intrusive; built for endurance on long runs; lightest model in its series; top-notch comfort; FF Blast+ cushioning for the first time in a GT 2000; PureGel technology for heel strikers; remarkable durability of the outsole; great value at $140.
negative_text: Who should not buy: If you're on the hunt for the ultimate performance daily trainer, the GT 2000 12 might fall slightly short, and it might not be the ideal pick for running on the hottest summer days due to its somewhat limited ventilation. Cons: Breathability could be improved; might require a brief break-in period; FF Blast+ lacks energy return; inconsistent traction.

positive_text: Who should buy: We've thoroughly tested the ASICS GT 2000 13 and we think it's a superb daily trainer for those with moderate stability issues seeking subtle support to align their stride without feeling restricted, loyal fans of the GT 2000 series who buy each version because this one feels familiar but better, and runners in need of a cushioned, durable, and stable daily trainer that’s built to last and is priced well below the Gel Kayano 31. Pros: Exceptionally durable; reliable; lighter than v12; heel-striker optimized; superior stability; can handle walking too; all-around comfort; Ortholite plush insole; optimized traction.
negative_text: Who should not buy: In our view, the GT 2000 13 may not be the best option for forefoot strikers due to its high drop and the PureGEL technology in the heel, and it lacks the fun factor some runners seek. Cons: Not for speedy runs; insufficiently padded tongue; upper lacks vertical room.

positive_text: Who should buy: In our view, the ASICS GT 2000 14 is a strong option if you have narrow feet and want a daily trainer that feels stable with every step, disliked the flat feel of FF Blast+ in earlier GT 2000 versions since the new FF Blast Max offers a better underfoot experience, or need a shoe that provides a supportive ride, excellent outsole durability plus versatility for everyday use. Pros: Improved FF Blast Max foam; outstanding outsole durability; stable design without feeling intrusive; reasonable weight; same price maintained despite upgrades; well-crafted, supportive upper; new outsole design improves flexibility; dependable traction.
negative_text: Who should not buy: We think the ASICS GT 2000 14 is not the best option for heavier runners or those who crave maximum underfoot cushioning, and the tapered toebox could be a limiting factor for those who need extra room for toe splay. Cons: Limited breathability; tapered toebox may feel restrictive; still lacks a truly energetic ride; PureGEL doesn't add much value.

positive_text: Who should buy: We recommend the ASICS Jolt 4 as an excellent training companion for beginner runners looking for a neutral shoe that's also good for walking and gym days, heavyset runners or those with mild overpronation who need more firm cushioning for a stable yet protective ride, runners in warm climates that need a comfy and breathable shoe, and those in the market for a wallet-friendly and durable daily trainer that can tackle easy runs. Pros: Extremely breathable; flexible and forgiving; feels stable underfoot; durable outsole; performs consistently in the cold; works well for walking or gym days; budget-friendly.
negative_text: Who should not buy: We found the Jolt 4's EVA midsole to be decidedly firm, so those who prefer softer cushioning from their daily trainers will be better served elsewhere, and it also lacks the responsiveness to facilitate fast-paced runs. Cons: Could be lighter; cushioning on the firm side; not responsive enough.

positive_text: Who should buy: This shoe focuses on customers exploring plated options for the first time, racers who want a speed-day shoe to pair with their MetaSpeed Skys, or runners who just want one shoe they can train and race in without the fuss of maintaining a shoe lineup. I recommend buying the Magic Speed if you like a firmer ride underfoot, not a lot of padding in the upper, and a little extra pop off your toes. Pros: Lightweight; affordable at $150; fast and nimble; smooth transitions; peppy toe-off; minimally padded; breathable; great grip.
negative_text: Who should not buy: Don’t buy this shoe if you are looking for a plush daily trainer with a soft ride or a maximalist shoe for recovery days; this is not the right shoe for someone looking to trod along at a gentle pace. Cons: Not for wide feet; not built for comfort.

positive_text: Who should buy: The second iteration of the ASICS Magic Speed should be on your list of to-buys if you're looking for a shoe that can handle tempo runs, workout days, and even some races. Pros: Snappy kind of fast; unbothersome plate; velvety smooth transitions; secure when cornering; roomy toe box; tongue stays in place; not a bank breaker; grips on wet roads.
negative_text: Who should not buy: The Magic Speed 2 is not perfect, and if a tippy-feeling heel is a deal breaker for you or if you want a bouncy ride, this ain't it. Cons: Heel feels tippy; it won't bounce you to your next step.

positive_text: Who should buy: We believe the ASICS Magic Speed 3 is an excellent choice for runners seeking a similar experience to the ASICS Metaspeed Sky+/Edge+ but at a more affordable price point, anyone searching for a feather-light shoe focused on tempo runs or faster paces, and those exploring their first carbon-plated shoe and aiming to spend the least amount possible. Pros: Perfectly balanced midsole; ample cushioning for extended runs; highly breathable monomesh upper; attractively priced; exceptionally lightweight; grippy and long-lasting outsole; great deal at $160 for a carbon-plated shoe.
negative_text: Who should not buy: The Magic Speed 3 may not be suitable for every runner; for those chasing peak performance, a carbon plate alone isn't enough because FF Blast+ doesn't quite make that cut, and it might not be the best fit for runners with wide feet due to its very narrow midfoot. Cons: Likely too narrow for some; midsole could use more energy return; upper could be more durable; needs better energy return.

positive_text: Who should buy: We've tested the Magic Speed 4 in the lab and highly recommend it for those seeking the dynamic feel of a carbon-plated shoe without the high cost, runners wanting a resilient training partner to the more expensive Metaspeed series from ASICS, and heavyweight heel strikers with a neutral footstrike who want superior cushioning. Pros: Enhanced midsole; exceptional grip; superior durability; awesome traction; excellent value for a supertrainer; colossal cushioning; carbon plate; ideal for marathon-paced workouts; fantastic upper.
negative_text: Who should not buy: While the Magic Speed 4 incorporates a carbon plate, its lack of a full-length superfoam and a lighter construction significantly diminish its appeal for those seeking top-tier performance, and its less-than-ideal support could pose challenges for runners who prefer a stable ride. Cons: Limited stability; low bounce; narrow fit.

positive_text: Who should buy: The ASICS Magic Speed 5 is a full reset that now suits beginners who want their first carbon-plated shoe that also works well for intervals and faster training days, runners looking for a lightweight, agile, and fun shoe at a more approachable price than most competition shoes, and fans of the Magic Speed 3 who were let down by version 4 and want a clear return to a lighter, more responsive feel. Pros: Feels like a Magic Speed again; full-length FF Leap foam; excellent ASICSGRIP traction score; outstanding breathability; nimble, race-inspired geometry; reasonable price; solid competition shoe for beginners; more flexible than super shoes; excellent lacing system.
negative_text: Who should not buy: While the lower price of the Magic Speed 5 may look appealing, we think it’s not the best option for serious racers, and runners who enjoy a sky-high, maximalist feel may feel underwhelmed here. Cons: Energy return limited by FF Blast+; maybe too extreme for heel strikers; not appealing for serious runners.

positive_text: Who should buy: Based on our lab tests, the ASICS Megablast is a great choice for runners seeking one shoe that can handle everything from daily training to interval sessions and races, those who love the unique feel of super shoe foams but prefer a plate-free ride, fans of the Superblast series who wanted something even lighter and more responsive, and marathoners seeking a super trainer built for long runs and tempo workouts that offers outstanding energy return and shock absorption. Pros: Outstanding energy return, exceptional impact protection, durable grippy outsole rubber, astonishingly low weight, performs at any pace and distance, breathable lightweight upper, high-quality materials from heel to toe, suitable for all footstrikes, top performance without a carbon plate.
negative_text: Who should not buy: We think the ASICS Megablast carries a sky-high price that’s hard to justify for many runners; you can get comparable performance for much less with the Adidas Adizero EVO SL, or go for the ASICS Novablast 5 if energy return isn’t your main concern. We also found that the Megablast’s stiffness might be a drawback for those who enjoy a more flexible, forgiving ride, and the Saucony Endorphin Speed 5 is a fantastic alternative offering excellent energy return, better flexibility, and a cheaper price. Cons: Extremely expensive for a training shoe, limited outsole coverage, poor toebox durability.

positive_text: Who should buy: We tested the Metafuji Trail both in our lab and on trails, just to find that it’s ideal for runners who loved the Metaspeed Sky+ but want that design adapted for trail running, works exceptionally well for neutral runners tackling trail races or ultras on moderate terrain, and feels like a perfect option for those seeking a fast trail shoe with a high heel-to-toe drop. Pros: Exceptional shock absorption, amazing ASICSGRIP outsole, high-end build quality, ideal for narrow feet, lightweight for a trail shoe, fast-rolling forefoot rocker, ideal for easy flat-ish ultras, outstanding upper with top ventilation, record-breaking stack height.
negative_text: Who should not buy: We believe the Metafuji Trail doesn’t justify its high price considering its good-but-not-great energy return, with FF Blast+ holding it back from true super shoe performance, and we recommend the HOKA Tecton X 3 or Adidas Terrex Agravic Speed Ultra instead. We also think the snug race-tight fit can be problematic because the toebox was restrictive in both width and height; for more room, the Nike Ultrafly or Kailas Fuga EX Pro fit better. Cons: FF Blast+ foam limits energy return, expensive, minimal stability, delicate upper prone to tearing.

positive_text: Who should buy: Buy the ASICS Metaspeed Edge if you are a 5K, 10K, or half-marathon racer that demands the lightest shoe on the market, or if you are a cadence runner who speeds up your legs at faster speeds rather than taking longer strides. Pros: Fast fast fast, insanely lightweight, breathable, great lockdown, grippy outsole, grippy laces.
negative_text: Who should not buy: I don’t think buying this shoe is a good idea if you’re struggling to make your rent payment because it’s a doozy at $250, if you are a stride runner who elongates your stride to go faster and should choose the Metaspeed Sky instead, if you want a bouncy energetic race shoe because this is stiff and stable, or if you don’t really need a carbon-plated shoe and could get the ASICS Magic Speed at a much lower price point. Cons: Spendy, stiff underfoot, durability.

positive_text: Who should buy: The ASICS Metaspeed Edge Tokyo is a remarkable step forward and a smart choice for heel strikers who find the Sky series unstable and want smoother heel-to-toe transitions, those seeking a more traditional super shoe with a curved carbon plate and excellent grip, Vaporfly fans looking for even lighter weight, a better upper and more shock absorption, and neutral-gait runners needing a versatile super shoe that’s explosive for a 5K yet cushioned enough for a marathon. Pros: Light as a feather, excels at any distance from 5K to the marathon, elite-level traction, new FF Leap midsole is super responsive, more stable than the Metaspeed Sky Tokyo, plush forefoot, excellent upper, classic rolling feel from curved plate, better than Metaspeed Edge Paris in every way.
negative_text: Who should not buy: The Metaspeed Edge Tokyo may feel too unstable for marathoners seeking control and an easy ride, and for those runners the Adidas Adizero Adios Pro 4 or Nike Alphafly 3 are much better picks. Another drawback is the rising price tag, and the Saucony Endorphin Pro 4 offers a more affordable alternative without losing the essence of a fast super shoe. Cons: Price increase, narrow fit, not stable.

positive_text: Who should buy: The Metaspeed Edge+ from ASICS is a great buy for runners who want a carbon-plated running shoe that can eat up marathon distances, or a responsive and lightweight racing flat that’s ready for tempo training and competitions. Pros: Speedy all the way, more cushioned for long miles, buttery smooth transitions, protective underfoot, hell of a snappy toe-off, super light, very breathable, nails the race-ready fit, slip-free lockdown, more durable than most racers, grips on dirt roads.
negative_text: Who should not buy: To trim down weight as much as possible, the ASICS Metaspeed Edge+’s upper is very very thin, which makes it scratchy, so if you want comfort to the max without added weight you might want the OG ASICS Metaspeed Sky. And with its $250 price tag, there’s no denying that the Metaspeed Edge+ is on the pricier side; if you want to save a few more bucks, stick with the older Metaspeed Edge. Cons: Scratchy upper, not for short runs, expensive.

positive_text: Who should buy: We pushed the ASICS Metaspeed Ray to its limits and found it’s a perfect choice for runners seeking a blend of speed, lightness, and plush ride in a racing shoe, forefoot and midfoot strikers chasing every second in 5K, 10K, half marathon and marathon races, those interested in the Adidas Adizero Adios Pro Evo series but turned off by the price, and marathoners who prefer a more flexible super shoe with a rare mix of flexibility and high performance. Pros: Unbelievable low weight, exceptional energy return from FF Leap, ultra-plush cushioned ride, less stiff than most supershoes, ideal for the marathon distance, excellent traction, well-designed forefoot rocker, not expensive for a true hypershoe, comfortable heel collar.
negative_text: Who should not buy: We think the Metaspeed Ray is not the best option for runners who dislike unstable rides because its full-length FF Leap midsole and compact dimensions make it extremely wobbly; for similar performance in a more stable package, the ASICS Metaspeed Sky Tokyo is a far better choice. Additionally, we do not recommend the Ray for heel strikers, as it simply doesn’t work for that running style, and the Saucony Endorphin Pro 4 or Nike Alphafly 3 are better options. Cons: Extremely unstable underfoot, not for heel strikers, aggressively tapered toebox, lacks the snap of a full-length carbon plate.

positive_text: Who should buy: The MetaSpeed Sky is ideal for the runner who has a slightly wider foot, is looking for top-of-the-line technology for race day, wants to lengthen their stride when running fast rather than speeding up their cadence, and demands not only speed but comfort, fit, breathability, and grip to carry them long distances at speed. Pros: Bouncy and responsive, fast, fantastic grip, ultra breathable, stable even at slower speeds, ultra-lightweight, perfect fit, superb foot lockdown, fits wider feet.
negative_text: Who should not buy: This shoe is not for the thrifty, at $250, and if you’re not an ASICS fan you may prefer the Nike Vaporfly Next% 2 or the New Balance RC Elite v2 instead. Cons: Not so durable, expensive, not for cold weather, no heel tab.

positive_text: Who should buy: After testing the Metaspeed Sky Paris, we are convinced that it represents a significant advancement for the series and is an excellent choice for midfoot and forefoot strikers looking for a top performer across any distance from 5K to the marathon, runners who tried the previous Metaspeeds but desired more cushioning, and ASICS aficionados who prefer the brand’s training shoes and have been waiting for racing options that match the top contenders on the market. Pros: Exceptionally lightweight design, world-class energy return, versatile for distances from 5K to marathon, all-weather grippy outsole, redesigned improved upper, incredibly cushioned, perfect for midfoot and forefoot strikers, fantastic durability.
negative_text: Who should not buy: We’ve rigorously evaluated the Metaspeed Sky Paris and are impressed by its performance, however its narrow heel and moderate drop make it less suitable for heel strikers, and the Saucony Endorphin Pro 4 offers a higher drop and a more stable rearfoot. We also believe that the Metaspeed Sky Paris may not suit runners with wider feet due to its narrow tight upper, and the Nike Alphafly 3 and Adidas Adizero Adios Pro 3 proved to be broader yet equally fast. Cons: Not suitable for heel strikers, less stable than Sky+.

positive_text: Who should buy: We pushed the limits of the Metaspeed Sky Tokyo and found it’s ideal for runners looking for a damn good super shoe that is lightweight, fast, and built with a super-grippy outsole, forefoot strikers with low to medium cadence aiming to maximize energy return from powerful strides, midfoot or forefoot strikers wanting a marathon shoe that lets them lock into a pace and hold it until the finish line, and fans of the Metaspeed Sky series looking for the best version yet. Pros: Light as a feather, explosive energy return, marathon-ready cushioning, fantastic ASICSGRIP outsole, generous toebox height, FF Leap is a true next-level foam, secure lockdown, excellent breathability.
negative_text: Who should not buy: We think the Metaspeed Sky Tokyo sacrifices too much heel stability, and with the narrow heel platform combined with the soft foam it creates an unstable ride; for a better option with the same bouncy feel and a more stable platform, choose the Adidas Adizero Adios Pro 4 or Nike Alphafly 3. We also believe that the narrow toebox can be too restrictive even for a racing shoe, and the Saucony Endorphin Pro 4 offers more room up front, better heel support, and a lower price. Cons: Higher price tag, narrow toebox, unstable for heel strikers.

positive_text: Who should buy: We suggest adding the Metaspeed Sky+ to your buy list if you’re craving a fast and fun carbon-plated running shoe for half to full-marathon distances, or a racing flat that can also double as a tempo-day trainer. Pros: Fast as hell, buttery smooth transitions, stable when cornering, very very breathable, heel lockdown is awesome, no more heel rubs, fits just right, grippy on wet roads.
negative_text: Who should not buy: Since the carbon-fiber plate of the shoe has been moved higher up to be closer to the runner’s foot, the ASICS Sky+ can feel a tad stiffer. You can opt for the first version instead—the ASICS Metaspeed Sky—or go for the PUMA Deviate Nitro Elite 2, which is more durable. Cons: Stiffer than before, outsole lacks durability, expensive.

positive_text: Who should buy: We recommend the ASICS Noosa Tri 14 if you are looking for a great performance trainer that can do a little bit of everything—slow workouts, tempo sessions, and races—and if you want an ASICS shoe that can be perfect for triathletes looking for an easy-to-slip-on shoe thanks to its amazingly comfortable upper. Pros: Do-it-all shoe, superior comfort, light and nimble, responsive and lively, versatile for slow and fast runs, no need for socks, great stability, efficient lockdown, eye-catching.
negative_text: Who should not buy: Look elsewhere if you like super soft shoes, because the ASICS Novablast 3 is what you are after, or if you want a grippier outsole, in which case the Magic Speed 2 is the better move. Cons: Firm at first, not as grippy on wet and dirt.

positive_text: Who should buy: We think the ASICS Noosa Tri 15 is a top choice for runners looking for a lightweight, non-plated option for both fast-paced training and racing, triathletes who need a shoe that can handle sock-free runs, track workouts, and interval training, and anyone seeking a running shoe with a unique design and impressive versatility for various running speeds. Pros: Extremely lightweight, well-cushioned for long runs, specifically designed for triathlons, unique design, breathable and comfortable upper, long-lasting outsole, suitable for sock-free running, excellent value at $130.
negative_text: Who should not buy: If you're after world-class performance, we believe this shoe is not for you because it lacks a carbon plate and PEBA foam, limiting its energy return, and runners who use a heel-striking technique should also think twice because the rearfoot is notably narrow. Cons: Underwhelming energy return, tongue might be too thin for some, not the best for heel strikers.

positive_text: Who should buy: After extensive testing in the lab, we recommend the Noosa Tri 16 for runners who love vibrant styles, those in search of a super-lightweight yet cushioned daily trainer that balances comfort with performance, and forefoot and midfoot strikers looking for a versatile shoe that does a great job during easy runs and tempo workouts alike. Pros: Lightweight, exceptional grip, attention-grabbing aesthetic, gusseted tongue, comfortable upper, great for triathlon, really versatile, fantastic price point, breathability.
negative_text: Who should not buy: While the Noosa Tri 16 shines in many areas, it may not be the ideal choice for heel strikers concerned with outsole durability due to its substantial amount of exposed midsole. Cons: Upper longevity, minimal tongue padding, still not enough energy return.

positive_text: Who should buy: Buy the Novablast 2 if you are looking for a neutral running shoe that’s heavily cushioned and soft, springy, and comfortable in all the right ways, especially if you like long slower runs, recovery runs, and daily training efforts where you want to give your legs a break. Pros: Fits like a glove, stable, springy and responsive ride, superb breathability, durable, great right out of the box, very soft and comfortable, perfect for long or recovery runs, reflective elements for night runs.
negative_text: Who should not buy: Don’t buy the ASICS Novablast 2 if you’re looking for a superlight and fast running shoe or a flatter fitting shoe with more ground feel. Cons: Really tall, untrusting grip.

positive_text: Who should buy: This shoe can do many things, and if you're eyeing a running shoe for jogs and long, slow runs, a daily trainer that's light and responsive for uptempo training, or a solid option for marathon runs, we think the Novablast 3 might be the one for you. Pros: Cushy feel for miles, breathable, light AF, ready for cornering, locks you in, tongue stays in place, mad durable, sets the bar for a do-it-all shoe.
negative_text: Who should not buy: Even though we find the ASICS Novablast 3 to be nearly perfect, it still has a few caveats: it's a tad narrow and it's more expensive now by $10. Cons: For narrow-to-medium feet only, stack heights higher than advertised, not enough energy return.

positive_text: Who should buy: We believe the ASICS Novablast 4 is an ideal choice for beginners looking for a fun, comfy, all-around shoe at a reasonable price, runners seeking a cushioned, lightweight option for short or long runs, and anyone needing a versatile daily trainer that effortlessly adapts to both slow and moderately fast paces. Pros: Enhanced outsole offering better grip and durability, improved upper comfort with premium materials, upgraded tongue padding, exceptional value at just $140, more cushion than ever before, accommodates a wide range of foot sizes, the most stable Novablast yet.
negative_text: Who should not buy: Though the ASICS Novablast 4 offers substantial cushioning, we think it doesn't quite match the ASICS Superblast in energy return, and it also falls slightly short in breathability, which might be a concern during the hottest days of the year. Cons: Not the best for hot summer runs, minor weight increase compared to v3.

positive_text: Who should buy: After testing the ASICS Novablast 5 in our lab, we’re convinced it’s an outstanding choice for runners wanting a versatile, cushioned, and comfortable daily trainer at a price below premium-tier options, fans of the Novablast 3 who felt the Novablast 4 was a downgrade, runners who want a maximalist stack height that feels surprisingly lightweight, and beginners who need a single shoe for casual runs and occasional tempo sessions. Pros: Improved energy return with FF Blast MAX foam, plushiest foam in a Novablast yet, keeps the same price as v4, higher stack height for extra cushioning, enhanced flexibility, lighter than its predecessor, best Novablast ever for wide feet, exceptional weight-to-cushion balance, works for short, medium and long runs.
negative_text: Who should not buy: The Novablast 5’s softer foam may not suit runners who favor a firmer or more balanced underfoot feel, and we also found grip to remain a concern, performing adequately on dry surfaces but falling short in wet conditions. Cons: Breathability could be improved, toebox durability.

positive_text: Who should buy: The ASICS S4+ Yogiri is an entry-level option in the carbon-plated category, and based on our lab testing and runs, it targets beginners or heel strikers searching for their first carbon shoe, runners who are not ready for full super shoes due to price or stability concerns, and those who want the feel of a carbon plate without needing top-tier energy return. Pros: Full-length carbon plate, very stable for a racing-focused model, exceptional traction, handles cold conditions well, heel striker friendly, doubles for interval training, inherits some features from the Metaspeeds, cheaper than most racing shoes, ideal for beginners.
negative_text: Who should not buy: From our perspective, we do not recommend the S4+ Yogiri as a primary racing shoe because its firm ride and modest energy return lag behind every real super shoe. Cons: Not a real super shoe, ride feels firm, very limited breathability, awkward price positioning.

positive_text: Who should buy: We think the ASICS Sonicblast is a great match for runners looking for a plated super trainer without paying +200 dollars, marathoners wanting a shoe that’s perfect for long runs and extended intervals while keeping their legs fresh, fans of the Superblast series who wanted a more compact and agile shoe with the same stack height, and runners with narrow feet who need a secure fit in a performance-oriented tempo shoe. Pros: Exceptionally cushioned, strong energy return, perfect for long runs, outstanding grip, top-tier breathability, fair price, more agile than most competitors, secure lockdown.
negative_text: Who should not buy: We think the ASICS Sonicblast isn’t the best choice for runners with wide feet because its narrow, tapered toebox feels restrictive, especially during long runs. Cons: Not for wide feet, stiff build limits versatility, could be a bit softer, misleading midsole branding.

positive_text: Who should buy: Based on our experience testing the shoe, the ASICS Superblast is an ideal choice for runners seeking a cushioned yet stable running shoe for countless miles, heavier individuals in search of a maximal training shoe capable of handling all paces, and those wanting a single shoe covering everything from daily training to racing distances from a 5K to a full marathon. Pros: Extremely lightweight, remarkably stable despite its massive height, works for both long and short runs, delivers a fun rockered ride, handles various paces effectively, ideal for runners covering high mileage, outstanding durability, amazing lockdown.
negative_text: Who should not buy: The main issue we found with the Superblast isn't a feature but rather its cost; at $200 it stands as one of the priciest daily training shoes on the market and many runners may prefer a more budget-friendly option. Cons: $200 for a daily trainer, wow!, subpar breathability, the outsole is a stone trapper.

positive_text: Who should buy: We put the ASICS Superblast 2 through all kinds of tests to determine if it truly is the ultimate Swiss Army knife of running shoes, and our findings confirmed that it excels for fans of the original Superblast, runners who simply want one of the best products on the market, marathoners looking for a plateless yet bouncy super trainer, and those desiring a single running shoe versatile enough to handle all distances and speeds from daily training to competition. Pros: Fantastic new upper, improved overall comfort, grippier outsole without cutouts, durability enhancements, excellent weight-to-cushioning ratio, supreme stability, works at every pace and distance.
negative_text: Who should not buy: We really liked the Superblast 2, but we do not recommend it for budget-conscious runners due to its steep $200 price tag. Cons: Slight weight gain, fit remains tight, minimal tongue padding, energy return is not on par with the price.

positive_text: Who should buy: In our opinion, the ASICS Superblast 3 shines for runners that enjoyed versions 1 and 2 but found them a bit too firm, want a super trainer that blends bounce, stability, durability, and strong grip in a single package, run high mileage and need maximum protection for muscles and joints while still enjoying an impressively lightweight shoe, and are willing to pay a premium price to get top-tier technology even for daily training. Pros: Exceptional shock absorption, improved energy return, softer FF Leap foam, lightweight despite maximal stack height, best traction we've seen to date, solid durability from top to bottom, keeps a very stable underfoot feel, versatile for multiple paces.
negative_text: Who should not buy: The ASICS Superblast 3 comes slightly short of the most energetic super trainers because FF Blast+ remains in the lower section of the midsole, and its high price can also be a deal-breaker for many runners. Cons: FF Blast+ limits energy return, unexpected price bump, limited toebox breathability.

positive_text: Who should buy: In our opinion, the ASICS Trabuco 14 will work wonders for runners who already enjoyed previous Trabuco models and want the best version yet, trail runners who prefer a low ankle collar that feels comfortable and avoids pressure around the ankle bones, and runners who want strong energy return in a dependable trail shoe without paying premium prices. Pros: Energy return upgrade with FF Blast Max, moderate stack for a more connected ride, awesome ASICSGRIP rubber, ideal for wide feet, strong value for the price, excellent versatility, no more GEL insert in the heel, stable ride despite softer midsole.
negative_text: Who should not buy: ASICS stayed true to the Trabuco DNA by keeping a moderate stack height and avoiding a maximalist design, so it may disappoint runners looking for monster-like cushioning. Cons: Somewhat heavy for its stack height, low heel collar can admit debris, lacing system can be hit or miss.

positive_text: Who should buy: We've come to understand that the Trabuco Max 2 from ASICS is a standout choice for those in search of a maximalist, super-cushioned trail shoe that provides unparalleled comfort on long distances, trail runners who log high mileage and need a shoe that minimizes leg fatigue on easy days, and ASICS fans looking for a trail shoe with the familiar upper comfort and running experience of their favorite road trainers. Pros: Plush FF Blast+ cushioning, airy mesh upper, fantastic for ultra distances, spacious, comfy and durable upper, enhanced grip, can handle some road running, hiking-friendly, fairly priced at $150.
negative_text: Who should not buy: From our lab data, the ASICS Trabuco Max 2 might not be the best bet for those seeking a nimble trail shoe due to its weight. Cons: Not winter-friendly, too high for technical trails, lacks ground feel.

positive_text: Who should buy: Following extensive testing of the Trabuco Max 3 both in our lab and out on the trails, we confidently recommend it for maximalist shoe enthusiasts looking for ultimate leg-saving cushioning on the trails, previous Trabuco Max fans seeking a warmer upper for colder runs, and fans of the ASICS FF Blast+ midsole desiring that plush feel off-road. Pros: Enhanced durability, exceptional plush cushioning, ideal for winter runs, ultra distance-ready, surprisingly stable, reliable grip, good-quality materials.
negative_text: Who should not buy: The towering stack of the Trabuco Max 3 might not be everyone's cup of tea because it’s a shoe designed for smooth paths and pretty much erases any sensation of the ground beneath your feet. Cons: Reduced breathability, increased weight, slight price bump.

positive_text: Who should buy: The ASICS Trabuco Max 4 is designed for forefoot and midfoot strikers who want a trail shoe with massive cushioning, runners seeking maximum protection and ground filtering on easy, non-technical terrain, and those who enjoy rockered, stiff-built shoes that stand apart from the average trail runner. Pros: Huge forefoot cushioning, full-length ASICSGRIP rubber, extremely stable despite its height, can handle any distance, handy tongue loop keeps laces quiet, well-padded comfortable tongue, rockered shape for easy transitions, ideal for flat easy trails.
negative_text: Who should not buy: We think the Trabuco Max 4 is not the right pick if you dislike heavy shoes because it feels quite bulky on foot. Cons: Not ideal for heel strikers, questionable outsole durability, heavy build, tapered toebox.

positive_text: Who should buy: We believe that the ASICS Trail Scout 2 best fits runners who are looking for a budget shoe for off-road adventures, a supportive trail running shoe for mellow days, or a trail shoe that can double as a light hiking and walking shoe. Pros: All-day support, durable, grippy on rugged and mountain trails, impressive heel lockdown, for heavy runners, can double for hiking and walking, astonishingly cheap.
negative_text: Who should not buy: If you're seeking luxury or a premium experience in the mountains, we feel that this shoe isn't for you because it’s a budget-friendly, all-purpose shoe without a fancy midsole, ultra-padded upper, or cutting-edge technologies. Cons: Narrow for wide feet, lacks responsiveness, heavy.

positive_text: Who should buy: In our view, the ASICS Versablast 4 is a great pick for runners on a budget who want a versatile shoe for daily use, anyone with narrow feet who struggles to find an affordable secure-fitting option, beginners searching for their first running shoe without spending too much, and those who love the Novablast design language but don't want to pay its price. Pros: Stable ride, cushioned for long runs, good performance-to-price ratio, ideal for beginners, solid outsole durability, great option for heel strikers, can be used as a casual shoe, inspired by Novablast DNA.
negative_text: Who should not buy: We found in the lab that the Versablast 4 has serious ventilation issues and a narrow toebox that won’t suit many runners, especially those with average or wide feet. Cons: Not for summer, upper durability concerns, narrow fit, probably a bit heavy.

positive_text: Who should buy: Take the Tree Dasher from Allbirds if you want a cozy daily trainer for easy short to mid-distance runs, a responsive ride for fartlek sessions, a modestly priced shoe for beginners, and a running shoe that can double for walking and gym training. Pros: Peppy on moderate tempos, stable on uneven surfaces, stays comfortable all day, versatile in use, great lockdown, stretchy upper, roomy toe box, durable outsole, uses eco-friendly materials, clean and attractive design.
negative_text: Who should not buy: Although it can pick up the pace, it is not the best choice if you are looking for a dedicated tempo shoe, and narrow-footed runners may want a snugger, cheaper alternative. Cons: Not for narrow feet, poor grip on wet surfaces, bottom-heavy.

positive_text: Who should buy: eco-minded runners and walkers who want a breathable stable shoe for casual runs, gym sessions, and everyday wear, especially those with wider feet. Pros: comfortable breathable upper, sustainable and carbon-neutral build, more refined than v1, stable ride, great lockdown, walking/gym versatility, and roomy fit.
negative_text: Who should not buy: runners wanting a soft lively performance trainer or narrow-footed users. Cons: can feel quite firm, roomy fit may be too spacious for narrow feet, and price is high relative to performance.

positive_text: Who should buy: We recommend the Allbirds Tree Flyer 2 for environmentally conscious runners who want a planet-friendly option with a much smaller carbon footprint to produce, neutral runners looking for a well-cushioned and comfy daily trainer, active folks wanting a versatile and durable shoe that can take on light trails and other fitness activities, and style-minded individuals looking for a sleek geometric design that is good for strutting about town. Pros: Environmentally friendly, breathable knit upper, good impact dampening, snug sock-like fit, accommodating toebox, secure heel hold, quite flexible and comfy, smooth and stable ride, works as a walker or gym shoe.
negative_text: Who should not buy: The Tree Flyer 2 may be prohibitively expensive for most runners, especially for a daily trainer; it is best suited to less intense runs over short to moderate distances, and runners wanting a better long-distance shoe or a snappier option for faster runs should look elsewhere. Cons: Quite heavy, pricey for a daily trainer, not so responsive.

positive_text: Who should buy: The Altra Escalante 3 is suited for runners who want a grippy and stable shoe for short to long miles on the road and a quick, energetic running shoe that is also stable on light trails. Pros: Comfy all-day, responsive ride, allows ground feel, delivers planted strides, grippy on both road and light trail, supportive fit, very spacious toe box, built like a tank, great for the gym and walking.
negative_text: Who should not buy: It is not recommended for runners with wide feet because the fit shifted from very roomy to rather snug through the midfoot, and runners wanting something lighter but just as speed-oriented may prefer a more race-ready alternative. Cons: Heavier than before, tight midfoot for wide feet.

positive_text: Who should buy: The Altra Escalante 4 is a fantastic choice for zero-drop enthusiasts seeking a stable shoe with awesome ground feel, for those needing a versatile low-drop shoe ideal for walking, some gym activities, and running, and for loyal fans of the Escalante series who were let down by the previous model because this version returns to beloved features of the 1.5. Pros: Minimalist, flexible ride, outstanding outsole, spacious extra-wide toebox, superb stability, lightweight, highly durable upper, recaptures Escalante 1.5 vibes.
negative_text: Who should not buy: As a zero-drop shoe, it may not suit runners with recurring Achilles or calf issues, and runners chasing a low-stack shoe that really pops with bounce may want something else. Cons: Poor ventilation, non-gusseted tongue, not ideal for long distance training.

positive_text: Who should buy: We highly recommend the Escalante Racer for minimalist enthusiasts seeking a zero-drop shoe that delivers a grounded and responsive running experience, Altra fans looking for a flexible lightweight option perfect for tempo runs, and runners who prefer a low-stack natural-feel shoe over more common maximalist models. Pros: Durable breathable upper, exceptionally flexible, minimalist natural ride, superior grip, spacious toebox, stable, really lightweight, authentic zero-drop design.
negative_text: Who should not buy: It is best suited for runners with strong calves and feet accustomed to zero-drop footwear with minimal cushioning, so anyone outside that group may want a more forgiving option with more cushioning and a higher drop. Cons: Minimal energy return, not the best for winter, non-gusseted tongue.

positive_text: Who should buy: The Altra Escalante Racer 2 is an outstanding choice for zero-drop enthusiasts seeking a fast nimble agile running shoe, runners wanting a minimalist shoe ideal for tempo efforts and short 5K and 10K races, and loyal fans of the previous Escalante Racer who value improved outsole durability. Pros: Enhanced toebox and outsole durability, incredible ventilation, grounded yet cushioned feel, true zero-drop experience, spacious foot-shaped toebox, superb stability, highly durable knit, premium perforated insole.
negative_text: Who should not buy: It is not the best choice for runners who frequently tackle cold temperatures because the massively ventilated upper barely retains heat, and runners in cooler climates may prefer a thicker and denser knit option. Cons: Heavier than previous version, lacks heel padding durability.

positive_text: Who should buy: The Altra Experience Flow is an excellent choice for runners previously hesitant about Altra's zero-drop design and welcoming a new low-drop option, Altra enthusiasts who have experienced calf or Achilles discomfort and want the slightly higher drop while still enjoying the roomy foot-shaped toebox, and those with wide feet who want a spacious daily workhorse that also doubles as a travel or everyday shoe. Pros: Broad appeal for various runners, exceptionally light, comfortable durable upper, spacious foot-shaped toebox, ideal as a travel shoe, superior stability, highly breathable, fairly priced, dependable grip.
