positive_text: Who should buy: The Altra Experience Flow 2 is especially suitable for runners looking to move from high-drop shoes to a low-drop option, or runners transitioning toward zero-drop shoes who want time to adapt; for those who tried the first version and found it flat or lifeless because version 2 is noticeably bouncier and more cushioned; and for individuals who prefer a flexible moderately stacked shoe that delivers a smooth natural ride over today's stiffer daily trainers. Cons: Poor outsole durability, not ideal for heel strikers, toebox height may feel slightly low.

positive_text: Who should buy: The Altra Experience Form is a solid choice for runners needing mild-to-moderate stability who want a straightforward low-drop daily trainer, fans of Altra's spacious fit and generous toe splay looking for a supportive all-day comfortable shoe, and runners curious about the brand or transitioning to zero-drop shoes who want a more approachable 4-mm drop. positive_text: Who should buy: The Altra Experience Wild is an excellent choice for runners who were previously hesitant about Altra's zero-drop design and now want a more accessible low-drop trail option, for Altra enthusiasts who have experienced calf or Achilles discomfort and will benefit from the slightly higher drop while still enjoying the roomy foot-shaped toebox, and for those with wide feet who want a spacious trail workhorse that can also handle travel and everyday wear. negative_text: Who should not buy: It will disappoint runners wanting a lively ride because the midsole lacks energy return, and the fit and shape are not ideal for narrow feet. Cons: Midsole lacks energy return, low heel collar allows debris in, non-gusseted tongue, not for narrow feet.

positive_text: Who should buy: The Altra Experience Wild 2 is a very good pick for heel strikers who want an Altra with a super-sized toebox and a smooth comfortable ride, midfoot and forefoot strikers who enjoy a truly wide toebox and want a stable versatile trail option, and anyone seeking an adventure-ready shoe that handles hikes walks and runs with ease. Pros: Extremely roomy toebox, very stable ride especially up front, beginner-friendly drop for Altra newcomers, flexible build with natural feel, impressive durability across the board, noticeably lighter than version 1, improved heel retention, better lacing system, same price as before.

Cons: Bad energy return, limited ventilation, non-gusseted tongue, not for narrow feet.

positive_text: Who should buy: The Altra FWD VIA could be your next shoe if you want an ultra-roomy toebox with plenty of space for natural toe splay, enjoy past Altras but want extra foam underfoot and prefer to avoid a 0 mm drop, and need a lightweight daily trainer for casual runs and everyday wear with a foot-shaped toebox. Pros: Exceptionally spacious toebox, lightweight considering its size, reliable traction, most heel-striker-friendly Altra so far, plush step-in feel, semi-gusseted tongue, stable landings.
negative_text: Who should not buy: The biggest drawback is disappointing energy return because the supercritical EVA foam felt lifeless and overly firm, and the drop also will not appeal to runners who prefer a classic Altra feel with more natural zero-drop geometry. Cons: Underwhelming energy return, midsole feels firmer than expected, durability could be better, actual drop differs from stated spec.

positive_text: Who should buy: After testing the Altra FWD VIA 2, we concluded that it is a good option if you want a well-cushioned daily trainer that avoids an aggressive rocker and keeps a more natural running feel, if you liked version 1 but wanted a softer and more comfortable ride, and if you are curious about Altra but hesitant about zero-drop shoes because the moderate drop makes it an easier entry into the brand. Pros: Much softer midsole than version 1, strong impact protection, very wide and roomy toebox, major improvement in grip, outsole is made to last forever, excellent space for natural toe splay, flexible build despite its high stack height, ideal for newcomers to Altra.
negative_text: Who should not buy: It may feel bulky for runners used to Altra's more agile models, and it is not recommended for runners facing scorching summer heat because the upper showed limited airflow in the lab. Cons: Small price increase, higher than expected drop, poor breathability, energy return still below average.

positive_text: Pros: Great for wide feet, superb grip on rocks mud ice and snow, stiff and durable, sheds mud easily, great water drainage, balanced and stable ride, removable Ortholite insole, great for road-to-trail transitions, extremely breathable, rock plate for technical terrain, gaiter-friendly, easy to clean.
negative_text: Cons: No ground feel, too cold for winter days.

positive_text: Who should buy: For neutral runners who want a go-to zero-drop shoe, the Altra Lone Peak 6 is a brilliant reliable option for anything from shorter speed workouts on the trail to full-on marathon-length runs. Pros: Fits true to size, excellent all-round grip, highly durable, great ground feel, feels like it makes you run better, great pull tab, gaiter compatible, brilliant lacing, roomy toe box, fully gusseted tongue.
negative_text: Who should not buy: You may want alternatives if you are looking for a technical trail shoe for ultramarathons or after something with a bit more padding in the midsole. Cons: Stain magnet, not enough cushioning for some.

positive_text: Who should buy: The Altra Lone Peak 7 is most suitable for trail runners and outdoor enthusiasts looking for a pair of all-around trail shoes for various types of terrain, excellent grip and lockdown in trail shoes, and adequate cushioning with a wide toe box for all-day comfort. Pros: Very wide toebox, protective midsole, superb lockdown, super grippy outsole, excellent for fast runs in the mountains, added heel cup provides stability, super comfy, easy to clean.
negative_text: Who should not buy: It may not be the best choice if it feels a bit over your spending limit, and runners bothered by limited colorways may prefer a different model. Cons: Colorways might be a downer, a bit pricey.

positive_text: Who should buy: We recommend the Altra Lone Peak 8 for zero-drop enthusiasts looking for a comfy and versatile trail runner that can tackle a variety of surfaces, runners who prefer flexible minimalist shoes that feel like an extension of the foot, adventurous spirits who need a stable and reliable companion for hiking or backpacking trips, and fans of Altra's signature foot-shaped toebox with plenty of room for natural toe splay. Pros: Responsive midsole, natural barefoot-feeling ride, low to the ground and stable, doubles as a reliable hiking shoe, breathable and durable ripstop upper mesh, secure midfoot lockdown, accommodating toebox, generously padded and comfy, dries quickly, gaiter attachment ready.
negative_text: Who should not buy: It is not the best choice for runners who regularly face wet or muddy trails or who want more durable traction, because the MaxTrac outsole felt less sure-footed in those conditions and did poorly in wear testing. Cons: Not so grippy in wet conditions, subpar outsole durability.

positive_text: Who should buy: From our perspective, the Altra Lone Peak 9 is ideal for Lone Peak fans who want the latest upgrades while keeping the same grounded feel and familiar roomy toebox, for anyone with wide feet seeking a trail shoe for hiking and running without breaking the bank, and for zero-drop enthusiasts wanting a low-stack midsole that skips the overbuilt max-cushion trend. Pros: True zero-drop geometry, extra-roomy foot-shaped toebox, Vibram option finally available, improved forefoot stability, flat-profile natural ride, secure lacing system, still a low-stack shoe, standard and wide versions, good durability.
negative_text: Who should not buy: It is not the best option for heel strikers because its zero-drop platform does not complement that stride, and runners seeking top joint protection or more cushion for longer efforts may want a max-cushion alternative. Cons: Feels really heavy for its size, stiffer than previous versions, lacks airflow, having two outsole versions adds confusion.

positive_text: Who should buy: The Altra Mont Blanc is made for serious zero-drop trail runners wanting a fierce-looking race shoe with great traction and a bouncy midsole to keep them going for very long miles, including 50 km plus, as long as they have a strong enough base to handle some stability issues. Pros: Phenomenal very bouncy ride, excellent grip, healthy 0 mm drop, looks great, breathable.
negative_text: Who should not buy: You may want alternatives if you want a more minimal zero-drop shoe or if you are after a super versatile trail shoe.

positive_text: Who should buy: After pushing the Mont Blanc Carbon to its limits, RunRepeat highly recommends it for zero-drop enthusiasts seeking a carbon-plated trail shoe for either short bursts or ultra races, forefoot or midfoot strikers who appreciate a Vibram outsole and a roomy upper in a performance-oriented shoe, and anyone looking for a carbon-plated trail shoe that still offers flexibility and comfort rather than stiffness. Pros: fantastic carbon-plated racing option for trails, zero-drop midsole, flat-and-flexible carbon plate, ample room for toes, comfortable and high-quality upper, excellent Vibram Megagrip outsole, flexible and comfortable for a plated trail shoe, roomy and anatomically respectful toebox, durable outsole, fully gusseted tongue.
negative_text: Who should not buy: If you were hoping for a super-wide fit, the Mont Blanc Carbon might not meet your expectations, and runners wanting a true trail super shoe with strong rebound may be disappointed. Cons: midsole lacks sufficient energy return, high price, not as wide as some may expect despite the foot-shaped design, foam gets much firmer in cold conditions.

positive_text: Who should buy: The Altra Olympus 275 remains a top choice for runners and hikers who spend most of their time on easy terrain, smooth trails, and long efforts and want a highly cushioned, foot-shaped, zero-drop shoe. Pros: high forefoot stack height, shallow 3.5 mm lugs that suit smooth trails well, strong option for long efforts, roomy Altra-style fit, light-terrain friendly cushioning and comfort.

positive_text: Who should buy: Add the fifth version of the Altra Olympus to your rotation if you want a protective and comfy shoe for long, slow runs on the trails or for backpacking and hiking. Pros: grippy on wet and dry trails, protective over long distances, comfort is a 10/10, toe box welcomes wide feet, gives out energy, breathable, stable ride, terrific heel lockdown, easy on and off.
negative_text: Who should not buy: For its hefty price, the Olympus 5 has serious durability issues, so runners wanting a shoe that will last a long time and cost less should consider alternatives like the Altra Lone Peak 6, and those wanting to avoid lace bite may prefer the Altra Superior 6. Cons: durability couldn't be worse, annoying lace bites, it's a splurge.

positive_text: Who should buy: RunRepeat believes the Altra Olympus 6 is an excellent trail shoe for adventurers tackling rugged, challenging trails who need exceptional durability and love unmatched toe splay, fans of the Olympus series willing to accept a slight weight increase in exchange for major improvements, and trail runners seeking a zero-drop shoe with outstanding stack height, top-tier durability, and superior build quality for long-distance runs or hikes. negative_text: Who should not buy: If you're searching for a lightweight, cushioned trail running shoe, the Altra Olympus 6 isn’t the answer, and runners prioritizing a lighter ride may be better served by alternatives like the ASICS Trabuco Max 3 or Brooks Caldera 7. Cons: heavy weight, minor price hike, low-rebound CMEVA foam, not ideal for those prioritizing lightness.

positive_text: Who should buy: The Altra Outroad is a true all-rounder for runners who want a zero-drop trail shoe that can handle almost all surfaces, feels extremely comfortable, and works from short runs to marathon distances. negative_text: Who should not buy: Runners expecting the usual very wide Altra fit may want to be cautious, and those wanting a highly flexible shoe in cold conditions may be disappointed. positive_text: Who should buy: The Altra Outroad 2 is a strong road-to-trail option for runners wanting a well-rounded zero-drop shoe that handles roads and some trails, offers a foot-shaped design, and comes at a fair price. negative_text: Who should not buy: The Altra Outroad 2 might not be the top choice for heel strikers because of its low heel-to-toe drop, and runners who want a more heel-striker-friendly road-to-trail shoe may prefer alternatives like the Nike Pegasus Trail 4 or Hoka Challenger 7. Cons: low drop may not suit heel strikers.

positive_text: Who should buy: Based on RunRepeat's analysis, the Altra Outroad 3 is a versatile pick for casual runners and travelers seeking an all-in-one shoe that transitions seamlessly from trails to roads and suits those who love zero-drop designs. Pros: seamless trail-to-road transition, versatile all-in-one design, spacious foot-shaped toebox, excellent comfort for wider feet, MaxTrac outsole grips well on varied surfaces, great for casual use and travel.
negative_text: Who should not buy: Runners wanting a highly specialized trail racer or a very technical-terrain shoe may want something more purpose-built. Cons: more of an all-rounder than a specialist, optimized for versatility rather than high-end technical trail performance.

positive_text: Who should buy: The Altra Paradigm 7 shines as a zero-drop daily trainer for both neutral runners and those with mild pronation, especially newcomers to the Altra brand who want added stability with a roomy, foot-shaped upper. negative_text: Who should not buy: If you usually run in shoes with 4-12 mm of drop, be cautious when switching to zero-drop shoes like the Paradigm 7 because a sudden change can lead to injury, and runners focused on tempo training may want a different option. Cons: higher price point, not optimized for tempo training, zero-drop transition may be risky for runners used to traditional-drop shoes.

positive_text: Who should buy: The Altra Paradigm 8 is ideal for runners and walkers who want a roomy, stable zero-drop daily trainer with softer EGO MAX foam, better comfort, and strong durability for everyday use. Pros: very roomy toebox with excellent toe splay, superb stability without being intrusive, softer EGO MAX foam, improved heel padding comfort, outstanding overall durability, comfortable for walking and daily wear, works well in winter, rare price drop from the previous version.
negative_text: Who should not buy: Runners wanting a lively, bouncy ride or a lighter-feeling shoe may want to look elsewhere. Cons: low energy return, not for heel strikers, heavier than its predecessor, still overpriced.

positive_text: Who should buy: The Altra Provision 6 is a perfect daily runner for those tempted by the promises of the zero-drop movement and for overpronators who want a dialed-in feeling from a stability-focused daily trainer. Pros: very light for a stability shoe, great for wider feet, ingenious stability system, incredible grip, more stability in the outsole, good cushioning for a zero-drop shoe, great to have in a rotation, good durability, consistent flex in all conditions.
negative_text: Who should not buy: Runners who do not want a wide forefoot, who struggle to get secure heel lockdown, or who rely on orthotics may find the Provision 6 less ideal. Cons: forefoot may be too wide for some, getting a good heel lock can be hard, orthotics may not fit properly.

positive_text: Who should buy: We think that the Provision 7 from Altra is a solid pick, especially for: Altra enthusiasts seeking a multi-functional shoe with added stability features like GuideRails; those in need of a lightweight, low-drop daily runner that provides a stable, firm ride; midfoot and forefoot strikers looking for a summer-friendly shoe offering top-notch breathability and great durability. Pros: Snug and secure lockdown; Foot-shaped toe box for natural toe splay; Stable without being intrusive; Excellent traction across various surfaces; Highly breathable design; Ideal for a wide range of paces; Performs well on light trails; Good durability.
negative_text: Who should not buy: We've proved that the Altra Provision 7 doesn't quite hit the mark for true zero-drop enthusiasts, as our lab tests revealed a heel-to-toe drop of 4.8 mm. This deviation might actually be a plus for some, but for the purists, we recommend the Altra Torin 7 for a more authentic zero-drop experience. Furthermore, we found that the midsole might feel somewhat firm for those who prefer a softer, more plush underfoot sensation. In our experience, if you're on the lookout for shoes that blend softness with some stability, the ASICS Kayano 30 and the Saucony Tempus are outstanding alternatives. Both of these options provide the luxurious, cushioned feel you may find lacking in the Provision 7. Cons: Midsole might feel overly firm for certain runners; Unexpectedly narrow upper; Limited energy return.

positive_text: Who should buy: We recommend the eighth edition of the Altra Provision for: Altra enthusiasts seeking a true zero-drop experience with enhanced stability, making it more accommodating than a typical neutral design; midfoot and forefoot strikers exploring their first 0-mm drop shoe—this model offers extra support, making it ideal for beginners; anyone in need of a low-drop daily trainer that provides just the right amount of stability for everyday use. Pros: Authentic zero-drop design; Exceptional stability at all paces; Anatomically shaped toebox; Competitive pricing; Durable, flexible outsole; Versatile for daily wear; Great for daily mileage.
negative_text: Who should not buy: In our tests, we noted that the Provision 8 may lack sufficient cushioning for marathon training. However, the Altra VIA Olympus 2, featuring a superior EGO MAX midsole and a higher stack height, excels during long runs. And despite its classification as a neutral shoe, it offers enough support for anyone with moderate stability issues. We also believe zero-drop enthusiasts seeking a more natural running experience might find the Provision series a bit cumbersome. If that’s the case, we recommend considering the Altra Escalante 4. While it’s not as stable, its lower stack and minimalist ride could be a worthwhile trade-off. Cons: Heel padding durability; Weight increase from v7; Needs more energy return.

positive_text: Who should buy: Take the Rivera 2 from Altra if you're: just transitioning to zero-drop running shoes; looking for a solid all-rounder that can handle daily training and tempo paces; wanting to buy a pair of running shoes that can double for gym training; searching for a roomy shoe that welcomes foot swelling after long miles. Pros: Responsive on faster runs; Stable on uneven surfaces; Comfortable for long days; Lightweight; Form-fitting wrap; Spacious toe box; Visually appealing; Soft and stretchy upper.
negative_text: Who should not buy: If you don't mind paying extra for a snappier ride that's more performance-oriented, we advise getting the Vanish Carbon instead. And if you want more stack for recovery days, and anything slower, the Altra Torin 7 is our suggested better option. Cons: Not a real zero-drop!; A bit slimmer than the average Altra; Firmer than the Rivera 1.

positive_text: Who should buy: We recommend the Altra Rivera 3 as an excellent option for: those looking for a comfy and well-cushioned introduction to the world of zero-drop running shoes; runners in the market for a shoe that boasts a flexible and natural ride that's forgiving on the foot; zero-drop enthusiasts who want a good amount of plush foam underfoot for daily runs. Pros: Great for easy runs; Superb comfort; Plush cushioning; Peppy and responsive; Flexible and forgiving ride; Unhindered and smooth strides; Toebox easily accommodates wide feet; Works great as a walking shoe; Can go sockless.
negative_text: Who should not buy: The Rivera 3's heel counter is very comfortable but doesn't provide adequate rearfoot lockdown which becomes a little prohibitive when trying to push the pace. While this can be mitigated by lace-locking the shoe, we also recommend the Altra Escalante 3 as a speedy alternative with a much more secure lockdown. We found a slight discrepancy with the stated forefoot stack height which leaves the Rivera 3 with a drop height of 1.7 mm. Zero-drop purists will be much happier with the Altra Torin 7 which actually has a negative heel drop of -0.8 mm. Cons: Lockdown needs a lace lock; Laces get untied easily; Not great for speedwork.

positive_text: Who should buy: We recommend the Altra Rivera 4 as an excellent choice for: neutral runners looking for a versatile zero-drop trainer that can take on just about any type of run; runners with medium-width feet who prefer some extra toe room to splay out; zero-drop enthusiasts who want a moderately cushioned and lightweight road shoe; those in the market for a flexible and comfy shoe that's good for walking, gym days, and general fitness activities. Pros: Good impact dampening; Responsive ride; Can pick up the pace; Roomy toe zone; Flexible and comfy; Pleasantly lightweight; Feels stable; Reliable traction; Great walking shoe; No break-in required.
negative_text: Who should not buy: The Rivera 4's performance in our breathability tests indicates lackluster airflow which makes the shoe a less-than-ideal choice for runs on hotter days. We recommend checking out the Altra Torin 7 as a better-ventilated alternative that won't feel as toasty come summertime. One annoyance we faced while testing the Rivera 4 is that the laces kept coming undone and forcing us to stop and tie them back up. This can be prevented with a double knot, or by checking out the also versatile Altra Escalante 3, which also comes in more colorway options, as an alternative. Cons: Runs a little warm; Unruly laces; Limited colorways.

positive_text: Who should buy: We highly recommend the Altra Superior 6 for: Altra fans looking for a low-profile trail runner that offers a natural, ground-connected feel; those seeking a trail shoe versatile enough to smoothly navigate sharp turns and adapt to varied terrains; runners who value a flexible, zero-drop design and need a shoe that's equally great at hiking and walking. Pros: Exceptional ground feel for a more connected run; Nimble and quick; Excellent breathability; Upper and tongue designed for maximum comfort; Durable outsole with reliable grip; Remarkable stability; Comfortable for all-day wear; Reasonably priced.
negative_text: Who should not buy: We discovered the Altra Superior 6 has a minimal stack height of just 22.0 mm for both the heel and forefoot, which we don't recommend for runners seeking underfoot cushioning, especially for longer distances. For those enchanted by the allure of zero-drop shoes but craving more cushion, the Altra Olympus 5 is a better pick, boasting a Vibram outsole for an added bonus. Furthermore, we believe the Superior 6 may not satisfy those yearning for a softer ride, as its cushioning leans towards the firmer side. If a more plush, yet low-drop shoe is what you're after, we recommend exploring the Hoka Speedgoat 5 or the Saucony Peregrine 13 as better choices based on our lab tests. Cons: Lacks a rock plate; Not ideal for long distances; The upper could be more durable.

positive_text: Who should buy: The Altra Superior 7 is a good buy for: runners coming from older Superior models who were waiting for clear upgrades; hikers who mix in occasional trail runs and want a zero-drop, low-stack shoe with a simple, natural feel; Altra fans who find most models too roomy and prefer a slightly more secure, less spacious fit while keeping the foot-shaped toebox. Pros: Excellent ground feel; Flexible and natural underfoot; Lightweight for a trail shoe; Solid traction; Good durability overall; Excellent value; Doubles as a hiking shoe; True zero-drop design; Gaiter attachment.
negative_text: Who should not buy: For many runners, the main limitation of the Altra Superior 7 is its modest cushioning and low shock absorption. We found in the lab that this ride can feel demanding on the legs, especially on long days. If post-run DOMS feel inevitable with this shoe, the Altra Lone Peak 9 offers more protection while keeping Altra’s DNA. We also think runners looking for a fully minimalist, barefoot-style experience may find that the Superior 7 still has slightly too much underfoot. From our point of view, its low stack sits in a middle ground that won’t fully satisfy purists. In that case, shoes like the New Balance MT10 or the Xero Shoes Scrambler Low could align better with you. Cons: Not ideal for heel strikers; Less roomy than other Altra shoes; Low toebox height; Foam feels really firm.

positive_text: Who should buy: In our opinion, you should get the Timp 4 if you're looking to buy a: trail shoe for both long and short-distance daily trail runs; soft yet peppy ride for light trails and moderately technical terrains. Pros: Grippy on wet trails and steep descents; Bouncy and responsive; Natural-feeling ride; Pillowy landing; Dampens impact; Wide toe box; Secure lockdown; Drains fast; Breezy under warm conditions.
negative_text: Who should not buy: Runners who want a cheaper alternative to the Timp 4 can buy the Altra Superior 6 instead. And those who want surefooted strides on highly technical trails can consider the Altra Lone Peak 6. Cons: Way pricier than the Timp 3; Lacks stability on technical trails.

positive_text: Who should buy: After extensive testing on the trails and in the lab, we're convinced that the Timp 5 stands out for: low-drop fans seeking a trail shoe that promises stability and minimal weight while adeptly handling diverse terrains; forefoot strikers with wider feet in need of a versatile trail shoe that can accommodate all their running adventures; runners keen on transitioning to zero-drop shoes and beginning their journey with trail running as their starting point; those eyeing the Timp series but holding back due to the absence of a Vibram outsole. Pros: Vibram Megagrip outsole!; Massive weight improvement; Exceptionally stable; True zero drop; Ample cushioning; Secure fit; Wide, foot-shaped toebox; Versatile.
negative_text: Who should not buy: If you're a fan of the Timp 5's features but you usually run in medium or high drop shoes, we suggest a gradual shift to avoid injury—starting with the Hoka Speedgoat 5. It's a middle ground in terms of drop that retains much of what we admire in the Timp 5, like the lightweight design and Vibram outsole. For those seeking a minimalist feel, we also think the Timp 5 isn't the best pick. Despite its zero drop, we found it quite rigid and too stacked. Instead, we recommend the Altra Lone Peak 8, which in our tests provided a more flexible and ground-connected experience. Cons: Needs a better foam; Not for heel strikers.

positive_text: Who should buy: The Altra Timp 5 GTX could be an ideal match for trail runners and hikers who: look for a zero-drop trail shoe that accommodates a forefoot striking pattern and engages foot muscles, calves, and Achilles; prefer exceptionally wide and rounded toebox shapes that accommodate all five toes comfortably; need a warm shoe with solid waterproofing and a gaiter attachment for fall and winter runs. Pros: Great shock absorption for long runs and hikes; True zero-drop platform; Extra roomy foot-shaped toebox; Excellent grip on mixed terrain; Premium Gore-Tex waterproofing; Great stability for uneven terrain; Highly durable Vibram Megagrip outsole.
negative_text: Who should not buy: If you have never worn a zero-drop shoe before, we highly recommend transitioning into this shoe category gradually and starting with a low-drop shoe (4-5 mm) first. The HOKA Speedgoat 6 GTX has similar features and cushioning as the Altra Timp but offers them in a 5-mm drop. And if you do want a zero-drop shoe but prefer a low-stack, grounded, (even minimalist-ish) silhouette, try the Altra Lone Peak 9. It is also available in a waterproof version. Cons: Heavier than average; Cushioning gets significantly firmer in cold; Frail inner lining.

positive_text: Who should buy: The Torin 6 from Altra is a killer choice for runners looking for: a max-cushioned running shoe that will mute out the ground feel; a daily trainer that will keep you stable and your foot locked in position; a zero-drop platform for a more natural foot position. Pros: Very well-cushioned ride; Stable for long miles; Upper comfort is spot on!; Highly breathable; Holds the foot in place; Heel hold is brilliant; Tongue is soft; Durable build.
negative_text: Who should not buy: If you want extra room for your wide feet, we recommend the Altra VIA Olympus, which is a much more spacious shoe than the Torin. Cons: Not the widest Altra toebox; Not a very energetic ride.

positive_text: Who should buy: We recommend the Altra Torin 7 as a great choice for: more experienced runners looking for a zero drop daily trainer; runners with broader feet who prefer shoes with a more natural, foot-shaped silhouette; forefoot strikers whole want a good balance between ground feel and protective cushioning. Pros: Pleasantly lightweight; Flexible and comfortable ride; Very breathable; Feels stable underfoot; Solid midfoot lockdown; No lace bite; Great traction over various surfaces; Suitable for wide feet; Can go sockless.
negative_text: Who should not buy: Individuals with pre-existing calf or Achilles tendon injuries should steer clear of the Torin 7 and other zero-drop shoes. For those runners, we recommend a shoe with a stable base and a higher drop like the similarly versatile Brooks Ghost 15. Pronating runners in need of a mild-stability shoe that also sports a zero drop should consider the Altra Provision 7 instead. Cons: Not ideal for heel strikers; Outsole could be more durable; Upper feels snug despite being wide; Low energy return.

positive_text: Who should buy: The Altra Torin 8 is a solid upgrade that feels great for: any zero-drop aficionado looking for a reliable daily trainer with long-lasting durability; those who felt the Torin 7 was an awkward version—this update feels better on foot with an improved upper and more secure heel lockdown; midfoot or forefoot strikers needing a shoe for easy-paced miles that can also double as a casual, all-day walking option; runners aiming to transition toward minimalist footwear and want an in-between step that still offers a true zero-drop platform. Pros: True zero-drop midsole; Foot-shaped, wide toebox; Excellent durability; Improved heel lockdown; Plush step-in comfort; Keeps the same price; Can be used for walking; Amazing for forefoot strikers; Improved outsole; Stable.
negative_text: Who should not buy: We think the Torin 8 isn’t the best option if you're after responsiveness—it gained weight over the previous version, and with its low-energy return midsole, that extra bulk feels even harder to justify. From our evaluation, both the ASICS Novablast 5 and Adidas Adizero EVO SL feel significantly faster underfoot. Both shoes lighter, snappier, and priced at a similar or even lower point. Cons: Weight gain compared to v7; Not for rearfoot strikers; Needs a better foam ASAP; Non-gusseted tongue.

positive_text: Who should buy: We recommend this speed demon of a shoe as a great choice for: Altra aficionados looking to add the brand's first super shoe to their running rotation; midfoot/forefoot strikers in the market for a carbon-plated, race-ready shoe that's comfy and protective enough for 5K to marathon distances; runners who prefer lots of traction from their tempo trainers for a stable ride that feels incredibly efficient on turns and corners; broad-footed runners in need of a snug-fitting toebox that isn't too constrictive on the toes for race day. Pros: Runs fast; Smooth heel-to-toe transitions; Lots of protective foam underfoot; Feels responsive; Propulsive toe rocker; Relatively lightweight; Stable during sharp turns; Lockdown fit; Roomy toe box; Accommodates wide feet; Breathable on hot days.
negative_text: Who should not buy: Our heel felt suspiciously higher than the forefoot when we first put the shoe on. This suspicion was borne out by our stack measurements in the lab which put the Vanish Carbon in the category of mid-drop shoes. This means that zero-drop absolutists still, technically, don't have a super shoe to toe the starting line, but will be better served by something like the ASICS Metaspeed Sky+ which is closer to the mark with a heel drop of 2.7 mm. The Vanish Carbon's Ego Pro midsole foam delivers great energy return but is definitely on the firm side of the spectrum. Runners who prefer a more plush ride from their race day shoes should consider the Nike ZoomX Streakfly instead. Cons: Expensive; Outsole is extremely flimsy; Could be lighter for a super shoe; Somewhat firm cushioning; Laces don't stay tied.

positive_text: Who should buy: We were eager to put the Vanish Carbon 2 through its paces, both in our lab and on the road, to determine its capabilities—and we're thrilled to recommend it for: Altra enthusiasts eager for a top-notch racing shoe from their favorite brand; runners looking for a comfortable, low-drop competition shoe that's also suitable for both tempo training and long runs; individuals frustrated by the restrictive toeboxes of traditional marathon shoes, as the Vanish Carbon 2 offers a unique foot-shaped toebox. Pros: Ideal for midfoot strikers; Unique racing shoe; Anatomically shaped toebox; Superior breathability; Full-length carbon plate; Excellent for tempo training too; Amazing tongue; Significant upgrade from v1.
negative_text: Who should not buy: We really liked the Vanish Carbon 2, but we do not think that it's a true super shoe—the midsole lacks energy return compared to leading models. And from our point of view, the $260 price tag should guarantee top-tier performance, yet it falls a bit short. If that's a problem for you, we suggest the Saucony Endorphin Pro 4 or the Adidas Adizero Adios Pro 3, both superior in shaving seconds off your time. The Vanish Carbon 2's outsole may also lack durability, making it less suited for dirt paths or gravel—common terrains for many Altra runners. For those who require a more rugged shoe, we think the Altra Mont Blanc Carbon is a better fit, designed to handle such demanding conditions effectively with its Vibram outsole. Cons: Insufficient energy return; Not a real zero-drop shoe; Durability concerns; High price tag.

positive_text: Who should buy: We recommend getting the Altra VIA Olympus if you: want to have a lower heel-toe drop runner in your rotation; need running shoes that accommodate broad or high-volume feet; are looking for a cushioned trainer that offers plenty of underfoot protection. Pros: Comfortable and premium-feeling upper; Roomy, unmistakably-Altra fit; Secure lockdown even for narrow feet; Smooth and responsive ride; Good for any distance at an easy pace; Effective, but not obtrusive, rocker; Copious amount of padding; Breathable; Absorbs impact well.
negative_text: Who should not buy: If you’re after actual soft cushioning on a zero-drop running shoe, look at the Altra Paradigm 7. And for zero-drop shoes that actually feel like zero-drop shoes, among the best is the Altra Rivera 3. Cons: Cushioning on the firm side, but a toss-up on how firm; Doesn’t feel like a zero-drop shoe; Substantial break-in period; On the heavier side.

positive_text: Who should buy: We're convinced that the Altra VIA Olympus 2 is a top pick for: Altra enthusiasts seeking the ultimate marathon training shoe, offering unparalleled comfort; those curious about zero-drop footwear but unwilling to sacrifice cushioning; individuals with wide feet desiring a shoe that perfectly accommodates their broad foot shape, thanks to its exceptionally spacious design. Pros: Incredibly roomy toebox; Authentic zero-drop design; Extremely cushy and soft midsole; Superb all-around durability; Cozy upper; Remarkably stable; Ideal for long runs.
negative_text: Who should not buy: While the VIA Olympus 2 boasts many strengths, we caution against making a sudden switch if you're accustomed to 8-to-10-mm offsets. For a smoother transition with ample cushioning, we suggest starting with a mid-drop shoe like the Hoka Clifton 9 before fully committing to a zero-drop option. Furthermore, we advise against choosing the VIA Olympus 2 for those who prioritize a lightweight and flexible shoe. Instead, the Altra Torin 7, with its featherlight build and the same zero-drop design, offers less stiffness and more agility. Cons: Limited ventilation; Tongue not gusseted; Still a bit heavy.

positive_text: Who should buy: Only buy the APL Streamline if you have money to burn or really desire to mix fashion and running together. For most people who don’t live on the West Coast, no one will know what APL is, but if you’re in LA and need to have street cred while you jog, this may be for you. For everyone else, plan on taking out a small loan to afford these. Pros: Enjoyable ride; Secure lockdown; Breathable; Grippy outsole; Lightweight.
negative_text: Who should not buy: Don’t buy this shoe if you are a real runner. There are plenty of training shoes that perform and fit better for easily half the price. If you: want a solid trainer that feels similar without breaking the bank check out the Brook Glycerin 19; want a true race shoe that APL seems to have tried to copy, it is better to just buy the original, the Nike ZoomX Vaporfly NEXT% 2, which is also $50 less; want something that is truly plush and comfortable to run in, you can’t go wrong with the Saucony Triumph 19. Cons: Price, price, price; Stiff upper; Lacing system.

positive_text: Who should buy: We're convinced the Addiction GTS 15 from Brooks excels in specific areas that make it a top pick for certain individuals. We think its: ideal for heavier runners seeking substantial support; perfect for those who prefer firmer cushioning and don’t mind added weight; excellent for runners concerned with pronation and outsole durability. Pros: Plenty of stability; Incredible outsole durability; Effective GuideRails technology; Accommodates custom orthotics; Grippy on most surfaces; Ideal for bigger runners; Highly breathable; Fairly priced.
negative_text: Who should not buy: Despite its strengths, we do not recommend the Addiction GTS 15 for certain runners. Those who prefer lighter footwear should steer clear of this model—instead, we suggest the Hoka Gaviota 5, which offers excellent support in a more manageable weight. We also think that runners who find firmer foams uncomfortable might struggle with the Addiction GTS 15. While firmness does typically enhance support, if you're willing to sacrifice some stability, consider the ASICS Gel Kayano 30 or the Brooks Glycerin GTS 21 as better alternatives. Cons: Lacks flexibility; Quite heavy; Firm midsole.

positive_text: Who should buy: The Brooks Adrenaline GTS 22 is a stability shoe that’s perfect for daily runs. It’s a perfect choice for medium-paced runs up to half-marathon length. The shoe is quite accessible with 4 different widths to accommodate most foot shapes. Pros: Promising durability; Stability for all directions; Improved aesthetics; Phenomenal grip; A firmer, faster midsole; True to size; Consistent in all conditions; Reliable daily runner.
negative_text: Who should not buy: The Adrenaline GTS 22 might not be your best bet if: you don’t over-pronate, while GTS tech is apparently fine for neutral runners, we found that you can shed lots of weight by choosing something like the Ghost 14; you want something softer, for longer runs (at the cost of speed), we prefer the Glycerin GTS 20. Cons: High price point for stability aimed at beginners; Midsole is too hard for some.

positive_text: In our view, the Brooks Adrenaline GTS 23 is a fantastic choice for mild overpronators who need a running shoe with more support than the average daily trainer, midfoot or heel strikers seeking a workhorse with a traditional fit, steep heel-to-toe drop, and a cushioned heel, and fans of Brooks looking for a reliable shoe that can handle easy and aerobic runs on various surfaces. Excellent stability without being intrusive, ideal for easy miles, specifically designed for heel strikers, outstanding breathability, availability in narrow and wide sizes, capable of handling tempo paces, and not expensive at all.
negative_text: Those with a forefoot strike might find the 12-mm heel-to-toe drop too steep and may prefer the Saucony Guide 16 or Brooks Glycerin GTS 20. Runners who prefer firm midsoles for additional stability may also want alternatives like the On Cloudflyer 4 or Hoka Arahi 6. The engineered mesh upper lacks durability and the shoe lacks cushion for forefoot strikers.

positive_text: After fully testing the Brooks Adrenaline GTS 24, we recommend it for runners who found previous Adrenaline models a bit lacking in cushioning because this version packs significantly more foam underfoot, heel strikers needing reliable support in every stride and looking for a durable, long-lasting running shoe, and those with mild stability needs who want a shoe that keeps the foot centered without feeling overly bulky. Stack height upgrade, reliable support, breathable mesh with oversized vents, price remains unchanged, available in 4 width options, good durability, plush tongue for top comfort, ideal for heel strikers, and a foam upgrade to DNA Loft v3.
negative_text: The Adrenaline GTS 24 is not the best choice for runners seeking a plush, cushioned ride in a stability shoe because the updated foam still feels noticeably firm underfoot, and the super-high drop can be a dealbreaker for midfoot and forefoot strikers who prefer a more natural transition. Firmer-than-expected midsole, high drop may feel too steep, toebox height is a bit low, and the non-gusseted tongue.

positive_text: As a shoe that entices runners on a budget, the Brooks Anthem 4 offers lightness and comfort. It is a simple, no-fuss running shoe that works for most types of runs including recovery runs, easy long runs, and 5Ks. Affordable, light and nimble, runs cool, true to size, and no hot spots or rubbing.
negative_text: Just do not expect the Brooks Anthem 4 to wow you because it is not cut out for performance-oriented running. Uninspiring ride and not responsive.

positive_text: If you're on the hunt for a shoe with a futuristic design and NASA-like vibes, the Aurora-BL delivers for folks in search of a standout premium workhorse. This head-turning daily trainer offers a plush and comfortable ride in a surprisingly lightweight package. Exceptionally plush ride, highly flexible design, distinctive eye-catching style, devours miles effortlessly, ideal for easy runs, fun ride, and cutting-edge design.
negative_text: The Aurora-BL sacrifices some durability. Durability concerns, lacks tongue padding, and a premium price point.

positive_text: The Brooks Beast GTS 23 offers top-notch stability and shines with exceptional support from its geometry and GuideRails technology. It also offers excellent breathability and a design meticulously crafted for heel strikers, with good volume in the upper for those with wide feet. Exceptionally stable, effective GuideRails technology, ideal for heel strikers, superior build quality, responsive DNA Loft v3 foam, durable and grippy outsole, comfortable and breathable upper, and good volume in the upper for those with wide feet.
negative_text: The Brooks Beast GTS 23 is far from ordinary because it tips the scale with massive weight. High weight can lead to leg fatigue, it is not versatile for faster paces or longer distances, and it becomes too firm in cold weather.

positive_text: The Brooks Beast GTS 24 fully lives up to its name with unparalleled stability and long-lasting outsole durability, making it a strong option for those focused on stability and value over speed or lightness. Wear-resistant durable outsole, comfortable upper, exceptional for heel strikers, doubles as a versatile lifestyle shoe, high-quality build, innovative tongue, world-class stability, and a stability-focused footbed.
negative_text: Its substantial weight and firm midsole create a ride that is anything but lively, fun, or energetic. Limited breathability, not for forefoot strikers, extra-firm midsole feel, and it is still too heavy.

positive_text: Enhanced maxed-out protection, excellent grip on dry surfaces, remarkably stable despite its massive height, ideal for heel strikers, perfect for road-to-trail transitions, ultra-comfortable for long runs with DNA Loft v3, lighter compared to the Caldera 5, superb heel support, and extensive upper TPU protection.
negative_text: Still too heavy, excessively wide platform for technical terrain, and too much exposed foam on the outsole.

positive_text: We think the Caldera 7 is a superb choice for runners who cherish the plush ride of Brooks road shoes with DNA Loft v3 and want a trail option for easy adventures, those new to trail running that want a superbly cushioned shoe, and maximalist shoe fans exploring new alternatives beyond Hokas that seek a stable ride. Tons of cushioning, plush DNA Loft v3 foam, best Caldera to date, superb upper ventilation, surprisingly stable, fairly priced, and can handle ultra races.
negative_text: Those who favor a firmer midsole would do better in other models, and those who prefer lightweight shoes will not find a pleasant experience here. Still heavy, outsole could be better, and not for technical trails.

positive_text: The Caldera 8 kept the same stable ride and excellent lockdown from previous versions, with dependable grip and a smooth feel, and it works as a small update from version 7 that keeps the same price. Outstanding stability, comfortable smooth ride, versatile on easy and moderate terrain, secure reliable lockdown, fair and reasonable price, dependable traction, and a cleaner more refined look.
negative_text: The Caldera 8 falls short of the max-stack promise because it offers only moderate cushioning and needs more foam underfoot to truly separate itself inside the Brooks lineup, while also remaining heavy and bulky. Still heavy, needs more cushioning, bulky overly wide heel, and only for slow paces.

positive_text: The Brooks Cascadia 16 is ideal for trail runners who want a stiff, protective shoe that is stable on varied terrain. It is amazing for long-distance aggressive runs, protects the feet extremely well, and may be worth checking out for runners on rocky and steep trails whose typical run is under 10 to 15 miles. The shoe also comes with a whole new midsole, updated upper, and nice features like a velcro gaiter attachment on the heel and a lace loop to tuck laces in during runs.
negative_text: Do not buy the Cascadia 16 if you want a light nimble trail shoe or if you want a soft cushioned shoe for long efforts. The shoe fills a narrow need as a protective stiff shoe, comfort could be better for really long efforts, it runs a bit hot, and at 10.9 ounces it feels too heavy.

positive_text: The Cascadia 17 remains a versatile marvel with upgrades like enhanced cushioning, top-notch breathability, and the innovative Trail Adapt system for unrivaled terrain adaptability. Adaptable across various paces, world-class breathability, comfortable for long runs, superior grip thanks to TrailTack Green, environmentally-friendly with recycled materials, excellent stability, rock plate protects the foot, manages technical descents with ease, and ideally designed for heel strikers.
negative_text: Even though the shoe is magnificent, it feels a bit weighty and its foam may feel slightly firm. Weighs more than v16 and the midsole may feel overly firm for certain users.

positive_text: Breathable upper, great value for the price, stability-enhancing Trail Adapt plate, good grip, amazing tongue, doubles well as a hiking shoe, fits most foot shapes, velcro strap for gaiter attachment, and designed with heel strikers in mind.
negative_text: Noticeable durability issues, midsole lacks bounce and softness, still on the heavier side, and no reflective elements.

positive_text: After testing the Cascadia 19, it is a solid choice for long-time Cascadia fans who upgrade every one or two years because this is easily the best version so far, runners seeking a versatile stable and highly durable trail shoe, and hikers or summer trail runners who value plush comfort paired with great ventilation.
negative_text: The Cascadia 19 is not the best pick for runners who prefer an agile compact-feeling trail shoe because its new ultra-wide midsole makes it feel more bulky than before, and it may disappoint runners seeking deep max-cushioned comfort for long ultras or recovery efforts because the midsole protection level is moderate rather than high.

positive_text: The Brooks Catamount 2 offers a stable ride in a lightweight package and a bouncy DNA Flash midsole with outstanding performance on easy trails like fire roads, even at uptempo paces. Impressively lightweight design, offers a stable ride, excellent traction on easy trails, ideal for midfoot and forefoot strikers, the SkyVault plate provides both protection and bounce, durable long-lasting outsole, and perfect for faster paces.
negative_text: The Catamount 2 is not designed for wide feet or technical terrain and is not the best for long distances. The upper might be too narrow for many runners, it lacks sufficient cushioning for longer distances, and it is not for technical terrain.

positive_text: The Brooks Catamount 3 blends comfort, stability, and lightness in a versatile trail shoe. It offers excellent ground feel with good cushioning, providing nimble yet stable performance across various terrains. Reduced weight from v2, enhanced flexibility, stable and secure ride, high-quality materials, suitable for all footstrikes, lightweight, fantastic Skyvault plate, and adaptable across various paces.
negative_text: Breathability and durability could be better. Poor upper ventilation, tight fit for some, and limited grip in wet conditions.

positive_text: The Brooks Divide 3 is a solid choice for trail runners on a budget who want a versatile shoe equally at home on occasional road runs, those who prefer a firm ride and need a hybrid shoe capable of tackling a variety of surfaces, and Brooks fans who sometimes venture onto easy trails with their road shoes and want something with a bit more grip.
negative_text: If you favor flexibility in running shoes, the Divide 3 might feel a bit too stiff underfoot even while walking, and if a plush soft ride is what you are after it likely will not hit the mark because the DNA Loft foam feels surprisingly firm. The forefoot may also feel too thin for forefoot strikers planning ultra distances.

positive_text: We recommend the Divide 4 to runners who need a lightweight neutral trail shoe with a roomy toebox, are new to trail running and want a versatile shoe that is also capable for road running or hiking days, and want a trail shoe that is well cushioned and ready to take on long distance efforts. Good impact protection, smooth ride, feels stable underfoot, works well as a hybrid shoe, suitable for hiking, secure lockdown, comfy fit, accommodates wide feet, grippy outsole, and easy on the wallet.
negative_text: Runners who frequent more rugged trails will benefit from a more aggressive shoe, and more advanced runners looking for a bouncy and energetic midsole may prefer a more premium option. Midsole feels a little dull, not for technical or muddy trails, and overall issues with durability.

positive_text: The Brooks Divide 5 GTX sticks to its road-meets-trail DNA and is a strong pick for poor-weather daily runs across gravel, dirt paths, and other light-trail surfaces. It is a good pick for anyone who wants a waterproof shoe for daily use that handles many surfaces well including easy trails, runners in cold and wet climates who need a dependable option for gravel runs during bad-weather days, and budget-minded heel strikers looking for a winter-ready shoe that is versatile and affordable.
negative_text: The Brooks Divide 5 GTX is not the best option if you want a true trail-focused experience because its road-inspired build falls short on tougher terrain, and it is also not the best choice if you really do not need trail lugs and mainly want a road shoe for wet conditions. In the lab the midsole feels a bit too firm and lacks bounce.

positive_text: The Ghost 14 from Brooks fits the bill as a shoe for all runners and works as a comfortable workhorse for easy days, tempo sessions, and even light trails. All about comfort, true to size, lots of padding, smooth ride, really stable even on light trails, great out of the box, built for longevity, and great grip.
negative_text: The Ghost 14 lacks breathability for hot summer days and is not the best for long distances.

positive_text: The Ghost 15 offers fantastic comfort and is a great choice for daily workouts, especially when cruising at slow or moderate speeds. It is an ideal match for heel strikers that tend to wear out shoes quickly in that area, and it remains a versatile option that is reasonably priced. Supreme comfort, optimized for heel strikers, no heel slippage, great grip in wet conditions, built to endure countless miles, impressive value for $140, great for both running and walking, and available in up to four different widths.
negative_text: We do not recommend the Ghost 15 for runners who prefer a lighter daily trainer, need a stability shoe, love high-stacked and plush running shoes, or are forefoot strikers because the shoe has an extremely high heel-to-toe drop and insufficient support where they actually hit the ground. Slightly heavier than its predecessor, could use some improvements in breathability, and not a good one for forefoot strikers.

positive_text: Loyal Ghost series enthusiasts who purchase the model every one or two years will find the v16 really familiar yet enhanced. Heel strikers looking for a high-drop workhorse for daily runs and a variety of other activities will appreciate the design and flexibility of this model. Runners who were not satisfied with the Ghost 15 due to lack of energy return will be pleased with the new DNA Loft v3 foam, which provides a significant boost in performance. Dependable, optimal for heel strikers, long-lasting outsole, great stability, improved responsiveness, versatile for multiple activities, and maintains affordable price.
negative_text: Its substantial heel-to-toe drop may not suit everyone—especially forefoot strikers who might find it limiting during their runs. Midfoot strikers might also find themselves on the edge, as the design doesn't cater particularly well to their needs either. It also lacks a bit of the excitement that some runners seek in a modern daily trainer. Tongue not gusseted, stiff heel counter, low toebox volume, and still needs better energy return.

positive_text: Fans who buy a Ghost every year can confidently proceed again, as it follows the same proven formula with only minor refinements. Those seeking a touch more cushioning will appreciate the added stack height and lower but still high drop from previous models. For runners wanting a single, durable shoe for two or three short runs per week and casual everyday wear, this shoe is ideal. Exceptionally comfortable upper, impressive outsole durability, softest and most cushioned Ghost to date, very stable for a neutral shoe, still a flexible and natural ride, doubles for daily wear, new drop adds versatility, traction is even better than before, and improved aesthetics.
negative_text: The DNA Loft v3 foam lacks energy return, and runners seeking bounce will likely be disappointed. The tapered toebox may feel restrictive for runners with wide feet. Outdated foam tech with low energy return, non-gusseted tongue despite price hike, still heavy, and tapered toebox.

positive_text: Runners who love the Ghost 17 and want a similar shoe for rainy days or winter training in consistently wet weather will do well here.
