negative_text: Who should not buy: Neutral runners, runners needing stronger stability support, and runners wanting to go fast. Cons: Grip is not reliable and there are durability problems.

positive_text: Who should buy: Runners seeking a snug yet comfortable fit for long distances, individuals needing moderate stability in a lightweight daily trainer, and Hoka fans who appreciate a stable experience in a durable shoe and do not mind a firmer midsole. Pros: Premium and comfy upper, still surprisingly light, subtle yet effective stability features, reasonably priced, excellent fit and security, and plush tongue.
negative_text: Who should not buy: Runners who need average or roomy toe space and runners seeking a dynamic responsive ride with more bounce. Cons: Limited breathability, low energy return, only for narrow feet, and grip could be better.

positive_text: Who should buy: Runners who found the Arahi 7 too firm and lacking in cushioning, anyone looking for a mild stability shoe that feels light underfoot, heel strikers who avoided the low drop of previous versions, and those wanting one shoe that is comfortable and stable for daily wear plus a few runs each week. Pros: Impressively lightweight for its size, strong shock absorption, wider fit than previous versions, outstanding durability across upper and outsole, Early-Stage Meta Rocker smooths transitions, higher drop suits heel strikers better, comfy and plush for everyday use, H-Frame stability system, and comfortable heel counter.
negative_text: Who should not buy: Runners training in hot climates and runners who need trustworthy wet-surface traction. Cons: Very poor breathability, grip needs to improve, still no supercritical foam, and minor price bump.

positive_text: Who should buy: runners who want maximum cushioning for easy days and recovery runs, plus walkers who appreciate comfort and stability. Pros: plush comfort, smooth gliding ride, stable platform, breathable build, soft upper, and good durability.
negative_text: Who should not buy: runners seeking a lightweight shoe, a roomy forefoot/midfoot fit, or strong rebound. Cons: tight midfoot, narrow toebox, heavy feel, and low energy return.

positive_text: Who should buy: It stays true to its comfort-oriented DNA, ensuring fans of previous Bondis won’t be disappointed; Hoka really set out to create a cushion-loaded powerhouse, and it fully delivers—making it perfect for slow paces and long runs; those seeking a premium shoe for casual running and walking with maximum underfoot foam will love the Bondi 9; its snug, locked-in toebox makes it an excellent choice for those with narrow feet. Pros: Endless cushioning; new supercritical EVA midsole; amazing step-in comfort; doubles as a walking shoe; stable for its height; built to last; excels at slow-and-steady paces; well-designed heel collar; lighter than v8.
negative_text: Who should not buy: The Bondi 9 isn’t the best option for runners hoping for a roomy toebox, as it remains best suited for those with narrow or average feet; those wanting a more versatile ride will likely be frustrated by the Bondi’s bulky, slow-moving feel. Cons: Not for wide feet; still feels heavy and clunky underfoot; drop measurement differs from stated; non-gusseted tongue.

positive_text: Who should buy: Buy this shoe if you want a super padded and plush recovery shoe with the added benefit of a stable, propulsive carbon plate; it’s spendy, but it’s comfortable and incorporates the latest technology in run shoes which many people will love. Pros: Well-cushioned; great recovery shoe; propulsive carbon plate; breathable; roomy toebox.
negative_text: Who should not buy: Don’t buy the Bondi X if you want a lightweight recovery shoe or if you want pure luxury and softness underfoot. Cons: Heavy; pricey.

positive_text: Who should buy: The Hoka Carbon X 3 is great for marathoners who want to finish the race instead of setting PRs; ultra distances in moderate tempo to faster paces; runners who want a smooth and easy ride during long runs; those who prefer a firm ride for training runs. Pros: Unobtrusive carbon plate; responsive ride; smooth transitions; stable; durable; sock-like collar; grippy.
negative_text: Who should not buy: The Carbon X 3 is a pass for runners who want a racer for short distances because it's on the heavier side; if you want something fast and light for such a distance, consider lighter options; if you want the ultimate energy return from Hoka, go for Rocket X2. Cons: Not for wide feet; rigid knit upper; not enough energy return.

positive_text: Who should buy: The Challenger 7 is for runners looking for a model that lets them transition smoothly from tarmac to single tracks on daily runs or ultras at a slow and moderate pace; beginners or advanced runners wanting comfort on almost any terrain except very technical trails. Pros: Lighter than before; effective lockdown; stable ride; superior comfort; plenty protective underfoot; excellent feel on roads; doubles as a walking shoe.
negative_text: Who should not buy: You shouldn’t get it if you want a fun and fast shoe or if you want a more durable outsole. Cons: Ride lacks excitement; not durable outsole; greater-than-expected drop; narrow toebox.

positive_text: Who should buy: The Hoka Challenger 7 GTX is an outstanding choice for Hoka enthusiasts looking for a versatile, waterproof door-to-trail running shoe; runners with narrow-to-medium width feet seeking a reliable Gore-Tex shoe for their rotation; fans of the Hoka Challenger series who desire a more durable option for just an extra $15. Pros: Fully waterproof and winter-ready; secure lockdown thanks to the gusseted tongue; amazing stability; provides a comfortable ride on roads and easy trails; ideal for long runs; generous CMEVA cushioning underfoot; surprisingly lightweight given its stack height; just $15 more than the regular version.
negative_text: Who should not buy: If you have wide feet, the Challenger 7 GTX might not be the best fit because its toebox is really narrow; it also isn't the top Gore-Tex shoe if you're more trail-focused than road. Cons: Lacks reflective elements; potentially too stiff for hiking for some users; higher heel-to-toe drop than anticipated; ultra-tapered toebox.

positive_text: Who should buy: The HOKA Challenger 8 is a strong pick for runners looking for a versatile road-to-trail shoe that handles various terrains and even works for casual wear; trail enthusiasts who stay away from highly technical paths and value long-lasting durability; heel strikers who previously avoided HOKA’s lower drops; fans of the Clifton who want a tougher, trail-ready version that feels very similar underfoot. Pros: Excellent cushioning with improved SA; flexible for a max-stack trail shoe; roomier than before; impressive durability; fantastic airflow for summer adventures; better suited for heel strikers; great stability for heel strikers; redesigned lugs for better grip; ideal for gravel.
negative_text: Who should not buy: The HOKA Challenger 8 is not the best pick for those after energy return because its compression-molded EVA foam feels outdated and lacks energetic bounce; it may also not please runners who value a terrain-connected feel because the maximalist midsole reduces ground feel. Cons: Outdated CMEVA foam; slightly heavier than version 7; price increase; non-gusseted tongue.

positive_text: Who should buy: The Hoka Cielo X1 is a fantastic choice for runners in search of an exciting super shoe who aren't concerned about a little extra weight; Hoka fans craving the brand's signature rockered ride in a supremely cushioned, marathon-ready design; those who prefer a snug fit and desire a super-rockered ride for both long runs and competitive races. Pros: Thrilling, fun ride; leg-saving midsole; marathon-ready; fantastic energy return; enhances forward motion; plush and bouncy PEBA foam; excellent durability; excels at long runs.
negative_text: Who should not buy: The Cielo X1 lacks the versatility needed for shorter events like 5K and 10K races; some runners may also be disappointed by its weight even for the marathon distance; runners with wider feet might not get the best fit. Cons: A bit heavy; some stability issues; pricey at $275; not the grippiest supershoe.

positive_text: Who should buy: racers who want a lighter, more breathable supershoe with a soft springy PEBA midsole and aggressive forward rocker from 5K through marathon distances. Pros: much lighter than v1, breathable upper, energetic dual-layer PEBA ride, powerful rocker, improved upper and laces, and strong race-day performance.
negative_text: Who should not buy: runners who dislike aggressive rockered geometry or want a calmer all-purpose trainer. Cons: the ultra-rockered shape will not suit everyone and the shoe is focused more on racing than versatility.

positive_text: Who should buy: marathon and road-ultra racers who love aggressive rockered supershoes, high shock absorption, strong PEBA rebound, and a secure race-ready fit. Pros: huge shock absorption, improved grip, excellent rocker-driven ride, secure fit, toothed laces, breathable premium upper, strong energy return, and lighter weight than v2.
negative_text: Who should not buy: runners wanting versatility, durability, or a more relaxed fit and less aggressive geometry. Cons: limited versatility, rocker may feel excessive, fit is very snug, and durability is poor.

positive_text: Who should buy: The Hoka Clifton 10 is perfect for runners who found the Clifton 9 undercushioned and wanted a thicker, more protective ride; heel strikers needing a stable, hard-wearing daily trainer with a comfort-first design; marathoners looking for a go-to option for long, slow miles that works for casual use too; those who skipped past versions of the Clifton due to a narrow toebox because this one finally fixes that. Pros: Superb joint protection; wider toebox improves toe splay; comfortable upper; stable ride despite its high stack height; works well for heel strikers; ideal for long runs; versatile for daily walking or jogging; Achilles-friendly heel collar design; traction improvements.
negative_text: Who should not buy: The biggest letdown of the Clifton 10 is its midsole because it still relies on plain CMEVA foam while rivals move toward higher-performance compounds; Hoka may have overdone the stack, pushing it past 40 mm; the extra foam also brings noticeable weight gain for runners who prioritize a lightweight feel. Cons: Needs a better foam ASAP; notable weight gain from v9; feels more Bondi than Clifton; non-gusseted tongue.

positive_text: Who should buy: Buy the Hoka Clifton 8 if you are looking for a plush daily trainer that’s soft underfoot with a smooth rockered transition; if you need a recovery day shoe or a long-distance slow run trainer; if you are looking for comfort, stability, plushness and to not break the bank. Pros: Comfy, comfy comfy; fits true to size; smooth rockered ride; great durability; grippy outsole; lightweight for a daily trainer; great lockdown.
negative_text: Who should not buy: Don’t buy the Clifton 8 if you are looking for a speed shoe for tempo runs, an ultralight or highly breathable shoe for hot summer days, or a carbon-plated race shoe; the Clifton is a luxury cruiser, so if you are looking for a sports car this isn’t it. Cons: Runs warm; doesn’t love to go fast.

positive_text: Who should buy: Get the Clifton 9 if you are looking for a cushioned road runner that can comfortably gobble miles; if you want a Clifton that can easily handle easy days, recovery runs, and some speedwork; if you need excellent value for money. Pros: Thicker and more energetic midsole cushioning; lighter than the v8; easy on the knees; durable upper; available in wide; mild, unobtrusive stability elements; best for easy days and long runs.
negative_text: Who should not buy: The Clifton 9 still has that narrow-leaning Hoka fit, so wide-footed runners may want to look elsewhere; for another daily trainer that’s comfy, cushy, and good to go from day one, there are more breathable alternatives. Cons: Snug fit; not enough bounce from EVA foam; average breathability; grip needs improvement.

positive_text: Who should buy: The Clifton 9 GTX is highly recommended for fans of Hoka seeking a waterproof running shoe for wet conditions; anyone looking for a cushioned daily trainer enhanced with Gore-Tex; runners hunting for a lightweight, waterproof shoe that isn't designed specifically for trail running. Pros: Exceptionally cushioned; comfortable and long-lasting upper; ideal for winter conditions; only $15 more expensive than the non-waterproof Clifton; still remarkably lightweight even with Gore-Tex; excellent for easy and moderate-paced runs; incredibly stable for neutral runners; ultra-durable Durabrasion outsole.
negative_text: Who should not buy: It’s not the best choice for summer runs because the toebox is completely sealed and lacks ventilation; it’s also better to skip it for mountain adventures since there are superior trail running shoes with Gore-Tex. Cons: May be too narrow for many; heel-to-toe drop significantly differs from Hoka's claims; not enough energy return.

positive_text: Who should buy: The Gaviota 3 is ideal for the heavier runner that needs a stability shoe for overpronation; it suits overpronators and heavy runners and works as a daily-run shoe rather than a fast uptempo option. Pros: Cushioned for heavier runners; tuned for overpronators; super stable, wide platform; extremely durable; plush, padded upper; roomy toebox.
negative_text: Who should not buy: Don’t buy this shoe if your feet are really narrow or if you’re expecting this plush trainer to be soft and bouncy because it’s stiff for lighter runners. Cons: A bit too stiff; heavy; tongue bunches up.

positive_text: Who should buy: Get the HOKA Gaviota 4 if you suffer from overpronation, love big running shoes, and need plenty of cushioning; it’s great as a marathon or ultramarathon training option but works pretty well for casual running too; it will please heavy runners looking for a lot of protection from the ground. Pros: Superior comfort; incredibly protective underfoot; very smooth ride; plenty of stability; breathable mesh; easy, effective lacing; great for long-distances; durable outsole; double as a walker.
negative_text: Who should not buy: Look elsewhere if you need a lighter stability shoe, want a less expensive shoe, or need a neutral shoe that’s ready out of the box. Cons: Requires some breaking-in; a bit expensive; heavy.

positive_text: Who should buy: The Hoka Gaviota 5 is an excellent choice for runners needing a stability shoe for wider feet; those who are looking for a comfortable-yet-stable shoe with nice arch support; Hoka fans wanting a cushioned, low-drop, long-distance shoe in their collection. Pros: Remarkably stable; breathable and comfortable upper; lightweight for its size; good stability option for forefoot strikers; ideal for wide feet; excellent for long runs.
negative_text: Who should not buy: The major drawback of the Hoka Gaviota 5 is its notably low drop, so runners who aren't keen on or simply can't use very low-drop shoes should skip it; if you favor a firmer and more natural feel for your runs, there are stronger contenders. Cons: Low drop might pose issues for heel strikers; performs poorly in colder conditions; not for narrow feet; midsole feels flat.

positive_text: Who should buy: The HOKA Gaviota 6 is a strong option for runners who have wide feet and struggled with the snug fit of many HOKA models; need maximum stability and want one of the most supportive platforms ever done; want a comfort-focused stability shoe for easy runs, recovery miles, or long walks. Pros: Roomy fit compared with most HOKA; excellent stability; improved outsole traction; good durability across the board; softer midsole than v5; good quality materials; keeps losing weight; 360-degree reflectivity.
negative_text: Who should not buy: The CMEVA midsole feels dull and low-energy, which limits the true potential of the Gaviota 6; runners seeking a more lively yet still stable ride should look elsewhere; heavier runners or those tackling long miles may also find the shock absorption underwhelming. Cons: Average shock absorption; high price for performance offered; outdated CMEVA foam.

positive_text: Who should buy: runners wanting one shoe for gym workouts, CrossFit, walking, and short daily runs, especially beginners who value comfort, durability, and stability. Pros: versatile use case, comfortable cushioning, durable construction, and a stable ride.
negative_text: Who should not buy: runners seeking a lively fast-feeling trainer or a shoe mainly for longer mileage. Cons: the ride is more practical than exciting and the shoe is geared toward short runs and mixed training use rather than speed or long-distance performance.

positive_text: Who should buy: The Hoka Kawana 2 is a superb choice for runners seeking a versatile shoe for walks, weekend getaways, and maybe even gym sessions; newcomers to the sport who want a multi-use shoe in case they decide against pursuing running as a primary activity; enthusiasts of the original Kawana, looking for the same reliable package with enhanced features that improve on its predecessor. Pros: Versatile for various activities; reduced weight from v1; comfortable fit; breathable, plush upper; secure lockdown with gusseted tongue; well-cushioned; fairly priced; super stable.
negative_text: Who should not buy: The Hoka Kawana 2 might disappoint serious runners because it's a bit clunky and lacks the engaging ride of more specialized running shoes; it is not recommended for performance use; while stable for neutral runners, it's not suitable for those with real stability issues. Cons: Bulky heel; narrow toebox.

positive_text: Who should buy: Buy this shoe if you are looking for a solid, sturdy, stable, lightweight trainer that can help you on your medium to medium/fast days. Pros: Glove-like fit; perfect lockdown; has an elf tab; lightweight; balanced; superb comfort; extremely stable; large reflective elements.
negative_text: Who should not buy: Don’t buy this shoe if you are expecting it to do everything well. It may be a jack-of-all-trades but it does not necessarily excel in any one area; it’s not as plush as the Hoka Clifton 9 for long recovery days and it’s not as fast as the Hoka Rocket X2 for speed or race days; it falls in an odd middle ground. Cons: Stiff for an everyday trainer; not a tempo shoe either; break-in period needed.

positive_text: Who should buy: The Hoka Mach 5 is one of the top-pick options if you are looking for a road running shoe that is lively and enjoyable for fast and easy runs, a lightweight yet cushioned trainer that can go the distance, and an excellent value for money. Pros: Fun and energetic; velvety smooth strides; runs and feels light; comfy for easy days; cushioned for long runs; absorbs impact; awesome fit; welcomes wide feet; reflective; worth the money.
negative_text: Who should not buy: Although it is a well-loved shoe, the Mach 5 also has its share of flaws, with the biggest one being the lack of rubber outsole, which leads to both a lack of durability and grip on wet pavement. Cons: Really lacks outsole durability; underperforms in cold weather.

positive_text: Who should buy: We think the Hoka Mach 6 is an excellent choice for enthusiasts of the Mach looking for the best version yet with an upgraded upper and more cushioning than ever before, runners seeking a lightweight daily trainer designed for narrow feet, and those who want a versatile daily workhorse that excels at long runs, easy days, and even tempo training or fartlek sessions. Pros: Really lightweight; fantastic outsole; exciting ride; highly cushioned; great for heel strikers; handles faster paces; superb lockdown; excellent value at $140.
negative_text: Who should not buy: We think the Hoka Mach 6, while bouncier than ever, still falls short compared to shoes with premium PEBA-based midsoles; if you are after maximum energy return for training, there are better choices. Additionally, we do not recommend the Mach 6 for runners with wide feet, as it tends to be narrow. Cons: Drop varies from stated; tapered toebox; thin tongue.

positive_text: Who should buy: The Hoka Mach X is perfect for runners who want a versatile shoe that can tackle a wide range of running paces, are looking for a tempo training shoe packed with incredible bounce and energy return, and seek a lightweight option that bridges the gap between a daily trainer and a race shoe. Pros: Lightweight and stable; works across all paces; comfortable for easy runs despite having a plate; finally a Hoka Mach with Pebax foam; well-suited for heel strikers; offers exceptional breathability; flexible and comfy despite having a plate.
negative_text: Who should not buy: While Hoka aimed to cater to a wide audience with the Mach X design, it might not resonate with every runner. The shoe’s dual-layer midsole, made of a soft Pebax foam with a firmer EVA foam, might feel uncomfortable to some; runners who favor tempo trainers without a plate and appreciate ground feel may not prefer it either. Cons: Official drop measurement (5 mm) is inaccurate; the $180 price point might be steep for some.

positive_text: Who should buy: After testing the Hoka Mach X 2, we believe it is an excellent choice for runners with low-volume feet who enjoy a snug-and-secure upper combined with responsive cushioning, Hoka fans seeking a premium super trainer for versatile runs from tempo sessions to long-distance efforts, anyone desiring an impact-absorbing shoe that works at both slow and fast paces, and those looking for a single highly versatile shoe to handle everything from easy jogs to occasional races. Pros: Excels in long-distance workouts; fun running experience; superior energy return; lightweight design for a supertrainer; adapts to multiple paces; surprisingly good durability; airy, breathable upper; ideal for tempo runs.
negative_text: Who should not buy: The Mach X 2 comes with a significant design flaw because its heel collar can dig into the Achilles tendon, creating discomfort and even potential bleeding, making it unsuitable for runners prone to Achilles irritation. We also think the Mach X 2’s low-volume toebox poses challenges for runners with bigger feet. Cons: Heel collar causes Achilles discomfort; price increase; small, tapered toebox; drop differs from stated.

positive_text: Who should buy: We believe the HOKA Mach X 3 is a strong pick for runners with narrow feet seeking a high-cushioned shoe for long runs and tempo workouts, HOKA fans who enjoy the Mach 6 but want a plated version with extra foam underfoot, and those who liked version 2’s ride but struggled with heel rubbing. Pros: Fixes the flawed upper from v2; deep cushioning; smooth, quick-rolling rocker; good energy return in the forefoot; exceptionally high stack height; keeps performance in cold conditions; reliable traction; surprisingly agile.
negative_text: Who should not buy: We think the biggest drawback of the HOKA Mach X 3 is its overly snug, tapered toebox, which restricts comfort and toe movement and limits its versatility, especially for runners with medium or wide feet. Additionally, the Mach X 3 does not quite justify its premium price given its modest energy return in the heel and increased weight. Cons: Terrible tongue; heavier than version 2; EVA foam in the heel dulls bounce; tight fit limits toe splay.

positive_text: Who should buy: We found that the HOKA Mafate 5 truly shines for trail runners who love long distances and need a shoe with outstanding cushioning and solid durability even at a premium price, fans of the Mafate Speed 4 who do not mind a bit of extra weight because almost everything else is improved, especially the midsole, ultrarunners looking for a versatile high-mileage workhorse for both training and racing, and runners who avoided previous HOKAs because of their low drop since this version finally offers a higher one. Pros: Even more plush cushioning; enhanced energy return; roomier fit than most HOKA models; durable yet breathable knit upper; built for long ultras; outstanding Vibram Megagrip rubber; same price as the previous version; stable for its tall stack; solid durability.
negative_text: Who should not buy: We think the HOKA Mafate 5 is not the best choice for runners who prefer a connected feel because its towering stack height and rigid geometry create a distant sensation from the terrain. We also believe that the Mafate 5’s heavy build makes it less ideal for some runners who want a faster feel. Cons: Heavy and heavier than version 4; tongue design could be much better; could be too stiff for some.

positive_text: Who should buy: We believe the Hoka Mafate Speed 4 is an outstanding pick for trail enthusiasts seeking a reliable and durable all-terrain shoe, devotees of Hoka’s road shoes looking for a cushioned trail companion, and ultrarunners who need a versatile high-mileage workhorse for their training and racing. Pros: Cushioned and comfortable; excellent grip across diverse terrains; outstanding Vibram Megagrip outsole; noticeably lighter than previous models; robust and long-lasting upper; adaptable to multiple paces; 100% ready for ultras; enhanced flexibility compared to previous versions.
negative_text: Who should not buy: If you are often tackling terrain with small rocks and debris, the Mafate Speed 4 might not be your best bet because its heel design tends to attract and trap debris. Cons: Ankle collar design favors debris intrusion; excessive foam exposure might be a concern; not for wide feet.

positive_text: Who should buy: After our batch of tests, we believe the Hoka Mafate Three2 is a solid choice for those seeking a durable retro-inspired shoe for walks and easy trail runs, runners who prioritize comfort, grip, and durability despite the extra weight and higher price, and individuals with narrow feet looking for a snug ultra-comfy fit with ample padding and effective ventilation. Pros: Unique mix of two shoes; serves as casual retro footwear; accurate heel-to-toe drop; exceptional Vibram outsole; outstanding durability; maximal cushioning; excellent stability; breathable.
negative_text: Who should not buy: We found the Hoka Mafate Three2 carries some deal-breaking features. Its substantial weight could turn away many runners, and we believe this shoe is too narrow for most, limiting its appeal. Cons: Lacks reflective elements; quite heavy; only for narrow feet.

positive_text: Who should buy: Based on our testing, we found that the Mafate X is a great match for runners who stay on easy trails or gravel roads and want maximum protection and maximum stack without worrying about the maximum price, heel strikers who need plenty of cushioning underfoot because the higher drop gives extra support in every landing, and those who want high-end tech from super shoes like a carbon plate or PEBA foam but in a trail package and do not need a fast or agile feel. Pros: Massive cushioning with nearly 50 mm of stack; strong energy return thanks to PEBA foam; Vibram Megagrip outsole; surprisingly good stability; solid airflow for summer days; high-quality materials from heel to toe; premium TPEE insole; boundary-pushing design.
negative_text: Who should not buy: We believe the Mafate X is not the best pick if price matters because it comes at a sky-high cost. We also think the heavy build makes the Mafate X too clunky for faster paces, with a slow and ultra-stiff ride. Cons: Very heavy on foot; too tall for technical terrain; heel lockdown could be better; high price.

positive_text: Who should buy: We recommend the Hoka Rincon 3 as a great option for runners of all skill levels looking for a versatile and lightweight daily trainer, speed-focused runners looking to challenge their PRs or train for a race, fans of long distances who need a responsive and well-cushioned road shoe, casual runners looking for a shoe that can convert anything from jogs to sprints to walks, and cold-climate runners who want a shoe that performs consistently no matter the weather. Pros: Light as a feather; smooth and responsive ride; suitable for long distances; performs consistently in the cold; extremely breathable; high-quality, durable upper.
negative_text: Who should not buy: The Rincon 3’s midsole does not feel quite as plush underfoot as it looks, so runners who prefer a speedy yet supple ride may want another option. Boasting a rather robust stack also means there is not much ground feel during runs, which can be a drawback for runners who prefer being more in touch with the road. Cons: A blister magnet for wide feet; thin tongue does not stay in place; lace bites; low energy return.

positive_text: Who should buy: We have thoroughly tested the Hoka Rincon 4 and are excited to recommend it for newcomers to Hoka seeking a cost-effective entry point into the brand, those who desired more cushioning in previous versions because it offers ample stack height for enhanced comfort during long runs, and runners needing mild support at a reasonable price because the shoe handles various paces smoothly and suits diverse training needs. Pros: Reasonably priced; enhanced stability; improved midsole over v3; superior breathability; still really lightweight; increased stack height; suitable for everyday activities; good traction.
negative_text: Who should not buy: We discovered that the Rincon 4’s toebox may not be the best for those who prefer roomy and high-volume shoes, as it tapers more than usual and offers less volume inside. Cons: Poor durability; low volume upper; weight increase.

positive_text: Who should buy: In our view, the Hoka Rocket X 2 is a match for runners with narrow feet still searching for that just-right racing shoe fit, those looking for a versatile racing shoe suitable for distances ranging from a mile to a marathon, and die-hard Hoka fans who have been exploring other brands for a racing shoe due to the absence of PEBA foam. Pros: Ultra-responsive PEBA foam; long-lasting outsole with extensive heel coverage; maximum cushioning; superb energy return; remarkably stable; suitable for all foot strike types; excellent traction; fantastic rocker geometry.
negative_text: Who should not buy: If you have wide feet, the Rocket X 2 might not be the right choice even if you consider going up a full size. Additionally, runners who are on the hunt for the absolute lightest racing shoe might want to look elsewhere, as the Rocket X 2 is on the heavier side. Cons: Bloody narrow toebox; heavier than the competition.

positive_text: Who should buy: You are the ideal runner for the HOKA Rocket X 3 if you are a neutral runner seeking a dependable super shoe with standout durability, if most super shoes feel too stripped down and you prefer added padding in the tongue and heel paired with a lively midsole, and if you want a high-performing shoe for long runs and tempo workouts and do not mind paying a bit extra compared to training-focused options. Pros: Fantastic durability for being a supershoe; responsive PEBA foam; built to handle marathon distances; rocker design promotes forward roll; heel stack flirts with the 40-mm limit; full-length, stiff carbon plate; higher drop benefits heel strikers; stable despite compact dimensions; comfortable heel and tongue.
negative_text: Who should not buy: From our perspective, the main drawback of the Rocket X 3 is its weight, and lighter options offer a faster racing experience. We also think that the rocker geometry of the Rocket X 3 will not suit everyone, especially runners preferring a flatter midsole who may struggle with its aggressive forward roll. Cons: Heavier than most competitors; tight, tapered toebox; aggressive rocker may not suit all runners; does not feel as fast as we hoped.

positive_text: Who should buy: We believe the Hoka Skyflow is an excellent choice for runners who like other Hoka daily trainers but seek more cushioning underfoot, a roomier fit, or both; those who found previous Hoka trainers like the Bondi lacking in energy return because this model features a new supercritical midsole for a more responsive ride; anyone in need of a highly cushioned yet stable daily trainer that excels on both short and long-distance runs; and Hoka fans drawn to the innovative Skyward X who prefer a similar, more affordable option without a plate. Pros: Great all-around performance; enhanced midsole from Clifton; versatile across multiple distances; solid stability; durable construction; breathable and comfortable upper; secure lockdown; grips very well.
negative_text: Who should not buy: We think runners who prefer a lightweight feel in their daily trainers might be disappointed with the Skyflow because it is simply too heavy to fit into that category. In our view, the Skyflow may also not be the best option for those seeking an ultra-plush running experience. Cons: Limited flex; could be a bit lighter; not the best for walking.

positive_text: Who should buy: We have rigorously tested the Skyward X and confidently recommend it for Bondi 8 and Bondi X enthusiasts seeking even greater cushioning and energy return, runners in search of a carbon-plated max-cushioned shoe that combines durability with top-tier performance, and marathoners looking for a premium long-run shoe that also works well for recovery runs if needed. Pros: Exceptional cushioning; innovative H-shaped carbon plate; tank-like durability; comfy knit upper; excellent stability; superior PEBA foam; smooth rockered ride; fantastic energy return.
negative_text: Who should not buy: The Skyward X might be a real super trainer, but it is a heavy one, so runners looking for a lighter max-stack shoe may want something else. We have also observed that this shoe is too narrow for many runners, including those with normal-sized feet who prefer a roomier fit. Cons: Excessively heavy; narrow fit; not for fast paces at all.

positive_text: Who should buy: runners looking for an affordable simple daily trainer for easy miles and walkers who want straightforward comfort. Pros: approachable price, comfort-focused daily-trainer design, and user-friendly easy-run feel.
negative_text: Who should not buy: runners expecting standout speed, bounce, or premium-level versatility. Cons: the shoe is more basic than exciting and is best kept to simpler daily mileage.

positive_text: Who should buy: trail runners who want to move quickly over rough terrain, cover anything from short runs to ultras, train for 50K races, and log big mountain miles.
